metadata.title: Cure vs chronic management, SoC staleness, outdated treatments, adjunctive and frontier agents: 100-disease database
metadata.description: Per disease: spontaneous remission rate, best-intervention remission rate, gap analysis (size + barrier type), last substantive SoC change, outdated treatments still in use, emerging adjunctives, and frontier/investigational signal. Diseases drawn from WHO GHE 2021 top-100 US DALYs and supplementary conditions relevant to OSMF research focus.
metadata.methodology: Barrier types: biological (ceiling is genuinely low with any available treatment), practice (better outcomes demonstrably achievable but not routinely deployed), access (effective treatment exists but cannot reach patients due to cost/infrastructure/policy), framing (chronic management is the cultural default despite evidence of cure-like potential).

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: 1.6-1.7% at 7 years on standard care (population-level cohorts UK and US)
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: 46-58% at 5 years post-bariatric surgery; up to 86% at 1 year with >15% total weight loss via intensive dietary intervention (DiRECT trial: 36% at 2 years)
diseases.gap_size: Very large
diseases.barrier_detail: Remission is biologically achievable and demonstrated in multiple large RCTs but almost never offered as the primary clinical strategy; default is indefinite pharmacotherapy escalation
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — ADA Standards of Care added formal remission definition (HbA1c <6.5% off all glucose-lowering meds 3+ months) and early GLP-1/SGLT2 use; prior first-line was metformin-only since ~2006
diseases.outdated_treatments: Sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia risk, weight gain, no CV benefit) remain widely prescribed despite GLP-1 and SGLT2 inhibitors showing CV and renal protection; insulin as early add-on before weight-loss intervention is attempted
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Berberine (equivalent HbA1c lowering to metformin in some trials), time-restricted eating, very low carbohydrate diet, resistance training as primary intervention (not adjunct)
diseases.frontier_signal: Tirzepatide achieving 15-22% body weight loss with high remission rates; semaglutide 2.4mg SUSTAIN trials; islet cell encapsulation (Vertex VX-880, Phase 1/2)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Not well-characterized as population baseline; routinely framed as irreversible in clinical culture despite contrary trial evidence
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Up to 93% had medications discontinued or reduced with intensive residential lifestyle intervention; DASH + weight loss reduced guideline-based medication indication from 51% to 18%; Lancet eClinicalMedicine 2024 meta-analysis confirms lifestyle superiority in motivated patients
diseases.gap_size: Large — primarily a framing and practice gap
diseases.barrier_detail: The 'requires lifelong medication' framing is contradicted by multiple trials when intensive lifestyle intervention is actually delivered; the gap is delivery infrastructure, not biology
diseases.last_soc_change: 2017 — ACC/AHA lowered treatment threshold from 140/90 to 130/80 mmHg (JNC 8 2014 had controversially raised threshold for over-60s to 150/90); first-line drug classes (ACE/ARB, CCB, thiazide) unchanged since early 2000s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Beta-blockers as first-line antihypertensive (no longer recommended in most guidelines for uncomplicated hypertension but still widely prescribed); high-dose diuretic monotherapy
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: DASH diet + sodium restriction (as primary, not adjunct); potassium supplementation (magnitude comparable to one antihypertensive); resistance training (BP reduction comparable to aerobic); device-based renal denervation (FDA-cleared 2023, Medtronic Symplicity)
diseases.frontier_signal: Renal denervation (Symplicity HTN-3 initially failed, SPYRAL HTN OFF-MED and RADIANCE-HTN SOLO showed 8-10 mmHg reduction); RNA interference therapy zilebesiran (single injection, 6-month BP lowering, Phase 3 underway)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: 11% achieve drug-free remission (DFR) at median 74 months; most cohorts show 10-15% DFR ceiling
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Early aggressive treat-to-target improves DFR rates modestly; some biologic-era cohorts reach 20-25% DFR; on-drug remission rates (DAS28 <2.6) reach 40-60% with modern biologics and JAK inhibitors
diseases.gap_size: Small for drug-free remission (biological ceiling is genuinely low); large gap between on-drug remission and drug-free remission
diseases.barrier_type: biological (DFR); practice (on-drug remission underachieved due to treatment inertia)
diseases.barrier_detail: Treat-to-target strategy is guideline-recommended but poorly implemented in routine care; many patients remain on inadequate therapy without escalation
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — ACR guidelines added JAK inhibitors with safety caveats; IL-6 inhibitors and IL-17 added to biologics ladder; treat-to-target explicitly endorsed; first-line (MTX) unchanged since 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Hydroxychloroquine or sulfasalazine monotherapy as long-term maintenance (appropriate bridging, not curative); COX-2 inhibitors as primary disease management; gold salts (essentially abandoned but occasionally seen in older patients)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) emerging in case series; dietary interventions (Mediterranean diet reduces DAS28 scores); HBOT in early case reports; photobiomodulation for joint inflammation
diseases.frontier_signal: CAR-T cell therapy (case reports in Germany, 2023-2024 showing sustained drug-free remission); regulatory T-cell therapies; anti-CD20 + tolerogenic approaches for DFR induction

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Relapse in 19-45% within 1 year of antidepressant discontinuation after remission; relapse risk ratio (off-medication vs on) falls from 3.69x at 2 months to 1.34x at 5 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: 55-80% remain relapse-free at 1 year after well-managed discontinuation; psychotherapy (CBT) achieves comparable remission with better durability; psilocybin-assisted therapy showing 29-71% response at 3 months in treatment-resistant cases
diseases.gap_size: Moderate; narrows substantially over time for maintenance medication question
diseases.barrier_detail: Guidelines push long maintenance durations partly because short-term relapse risk is high, but long-term maintenance evidence is weaker than commonly assumed; psychotherapy significantly underutilized relative to medication
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved esketamine (Spravato) nasal spray for TRD; 2023 FDA approved psilocybin-assisted therapy pathway (Australian TGA 2023, US FDA Breakthrough designation); first-line SSRIs unchanged since early 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) in routine first-line use; benzodiazepine co-prescribing as long-term adjunct (dependence risk, no antidepressant effect); antipsychotic augmentation before psychotherapy is attempted
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (meta-analysis: comparable to SSRI for mild-moderate depression); phototherapy (for non-seasonal depression, underutilized); anti-inflammatory interventions (NSAIDs, omega-3) for inflammatory subtype; vagus nerve stimulation (FDA-cleared 2005, coverage finally added 2023)
diseases.frontier_signal: Psilocybin-assisted therapy (COMP360, FDA Breakthrough); MDMA-assisted therapy (FDA review ongoing); rapid-acting glutamatergic agents (ketamine analogs); transcranial magnetic stimulation (expanded protocols)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: 60-85% achieve seizure freedom on medication; of those, 50-75% remain seizure-free after withdrawal (varies by syndrome and duration of freedom)
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Not clearly higher than on-medication rates -- the real gap is behavioral/practice
diseases.gap_size: Small biological gap; large practice gap
diseases.barrier_detail: Only 26-45% of seizure-free patients who are candidates for medication withdrawal ever attempt it; physician and patient reluctance vastly exceeds biological risk
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved cenobamate, fenfluramine for Dravet; first-line ASM (levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate) unchanged since 1990s-2000s; ketogenic diet formally endorsed in 2018 AES guidelines
diseases.outdated_treatments: Phenobarbital as first-line in adults (cognitive effects, dependence; still >50% of first-line therapy in low-income countries and some US rural settings); phenytoin loading in status epilepticus (fosphenytoin preferred); carbamazepine without therapeutic drug monitoring
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Ketogenic diet (proven for drug-resistant epilepsy, underutilized); vagus nerve stimulation; responsive neurostimulation (RNS); low-glycemic index treatment; CBD (Epidiolex approved, broader CBD products under study)
diseases.frontier_signal: Gene therapy for SCN1A (Dravet syndrome) in early trials; antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) for genetic epilepsies; optogenetic approaches; closed-loop neurostimulation with seizure prediction

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous clearance in <30% of acute cases; chronic infection rarely clears without treatment (<1%/year)
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: >95% cured (sustained virologic response at 12 weeks) with 8-12 week courses of direct-acting antivirals (DAAs)
diseases.gap_size: Near-zero biological gap; large access/utilization gap
diseases.barrier_detail: Only 65% of eligible patients with private insurance received DAA treatment as of 2020; rates declining since 2019; Medicaid restrictions remain in many states; $20,000+ drug cost for generics unavailable in US
diseases.last_soc_change: 2014-2016 — DAA revolution (sofosbuvir 2013, ledipasvir/sofosbuvir 2014, pan-genotypic regimens 2016-2017); prior interferon-ribavirin standard unchanged since ~2001 with <50% efficacy and severe side effects
diseases.outdated_treatments: Interferon-based regimens (now essentially obsolete but occasionally prescribed in settings with limited DAA access); ribavirin-only maintenance (no antiviral effect alone)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Harm reduction integration (needle exchange + DAA treatment as combined strategy); primary-care-based treatment (task-shifting from hepatologist to GP dramatically increases uptake in trials)
diseases.frontier_signal: HCV vaccine candidates (multiple Phase 1/2, MRC/NIH); cure for HCV reactivation in immunocompromised; ultra-short (4-week) DAA regimens under investigation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Disease course unchanged in ~85% of patients at 10 years on standard care; ~23% (CD) and ~12% (UC) relapse within 12 months of thiopurine withdrawal in sustained remission
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Anti-IL-23 biologics (mirikizumab, risankizumab) maintain steroid-free remission in >60% at 2 years — but on the drug, not drug-free
diseases.gap_size: Large -- but between 'no remission' and 'on-drug remission'; drug-free remission remains rare
diseases.barrier_detail: Biologic revolution has raised on-drug remission without meaningfully improving drug-free remission rates; true disease modification (cure-like durability off treatment) remains elusive
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — IL-23 inhibitors added to UC/CD ladder; 2018-2022 JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) added; anti-TNF (infliximab) first-line since 1998, essentially unchanged as backbone
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term corticosteroids as maintenance (bone density loss, metabolic effects; guidelines explicitly recommend against steroid-dependent maintenance); azathioprine monotherapy without biologic in moderate-severe disease
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) — RCT evidence in UC showing ~32% remission vs 9% placebo; exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) in pediatric Crohn's comparable to steroids; low-FODMAP diet; exclusive dietary therapy trials underway
diseases.frontier_signal: FMT (multiple Phase 3 underway); stem cell transplantation for refractory CD (ASTIC trial); selective JAK1 inhibitors (filgotinib, upadacitinib); gut-selective S1P modulators; anti-TL1A (tulisokibart, Phase 3)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: 26% of childhood-onset asthma in clinical remission by adulthood; <20% adult-onset achieves sustained remission; 65% long-term remission in mild-moderate vs 16% in severe
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Biologics (anti-IL-5, anti-IL-4/13, anti-IgE) achieve near-zero exacerbation rates in eligible severe patients; allergen immunotherapy (AIT) achieves sustained drug-free remission in allergic asthma in ~30-40% of treated patients
diseases.gap_size: Moderate; highly severity-dependent; large practice gap for AIT
diseases.barrier_detail: Allergen immunotherapy is the only disease-modifying treatment for allergic asthma and is underused relative to guidelines; biological phenotyping (T2-high vs T2-low) drives outcomes more than treatment intensity alone
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — GINA guidelines introduced 'anti-inflammatory reliever' strategy (ICS-formoterol as reliever replacing SABA monotherapy); biologics formally endorsed as step 5 since ~2016; ICS as first-line unchanged since 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) monotherapy as reliever without ICS (GINA 2019 explicitly removed SABA-only from recommended options after evidence of harm); oral corticosteroid 'burst' as go-to for exacerbations without underlying biologic assessment
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Bronchial thermoplasty (FDA approved, underutilized in severe non-biologic-eligible patients); vitamin D supplementation (meta-analysis: reduces exacerbation risk 50% in deficient patients); dietary intervention (Mediterranean diet reduces exacerbation frequency)
diseases.frontier_signal: Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) for unselected severe asthma regardless of eosinophil count; dupilumab extended to children 6+ months; CRISPR approaches to IL-4 receptor editing in murine models

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Effectively zero — airflow obstruction is irreversible by definition in COPD; FEV1 decline continues at 30-60 mL/year on standard care
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Smoking cessation slows FEV1 decline to near-normal aging rate (the only intervention that does this); lung volume reduction surgery (LVRS) improves FEV1 15-20% in selected emphysema patients; 52% of COPD patients achieve clinically meaningful improvement after pulmonary rehabilitation
diseases.gap_size: Large for disease modification (smoking cessation the only real lever); moderate for functional outcomes (pulmonary rehab underused)
diseases.barrier_detail: Biological limit: structural lung damage is not reversible with any current pharmacotherapy. Practice gap: pulmonary rehabilitation achieves clinically meaningful improvement in 52% of patients but fewer than 2% of eligible COPD patients in the US receive it. 26% of patients with a COPD diagnosis have no evidence of airflow obstruction on spirometry (misdiagnosed, overtreated)
diseases.last_soc_change: 2024 — GOLD report added dupilumab for Type 2 inflammation subgroup (eosinophilic COPD); triple therapy (LABA/LAMA/ICS) endpoint data (IMPACT, ETHOS trials 2018-2020); LABA/LAMA dual bronchodilation as backbone unchanged since ~2006
diseases.outdated_treatments: ICS monotherapy without LABA/LAMA in non-eosinophilic COPD (increases pneumonia risk, no mortality benefit); mucolytic agents (e.g., NAC) at standard doses (high-dose emerging evidence, but standard dose RCTs largely negative); long-term oral corticosteroids in stable COPD
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: High-dose NAC (1800 mg/day vs standard 600 mg) showing FEV1 benefit in Chinese cohorts; endobronchial valve placement (FDA approved, Zephyr valve) for hyperinflation in selected patients; airway clearance devices
diseases.frontier_signal: Dupilumab in eosinophilic COPD (BOREAS/NOTUS trials positive, 2023-2024); tezepelumab Phase 3 in COPD; lung regeneration approaches; stem cell therapy (early Phase 1); senolytic therapy (targeting lung senescent cells — navitoclax, dasatinib+quercetin)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Symptom recurrence in 70-80% within 6 months of PPI discontinuation in erosive GERD; highly variable in non-erosive disease; ~20% of GERD is nonerosive, where acid suppression is less effective
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Weight loss of >10% reduces symptoms in 65-81% of obese patients; laparoscopic anti-reflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) achieves long-term symptom control in 85-92% and eliminates daily PPI need in majority; bariatric surgery in obese patients near-eliminates GERD
diseases.gap_size: Large for lifestyle/surgical approaches vs standard chronic PPI use
diseases.barrier_detail: AGA 2022 guidelines explicitly recommend PPI de-escalation or discontinuation in patients without erosive esophagitis or Barrett's — yet 84 million monthly Medicare PPI claims in 2019 suggest massive overprescription; most patients are never offered surgical or intensive lifestyle options
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — AGA Clinical Practice Update on PPI de-prescribing (explicit guidance to attempt discontinuation in low-risk patients); 2022 vonoprazan (P-CAB) approved as alternative; PPI-first strategy unchanged since 1989
diseases.outdated_treatments: Indefinite daily PPI in uncomplicated, nonerosive GERD (80% of GERD cases do not meet criteria for long-term PPI; 70-80% of GERD patients on PPIs have no documented appropriate indication); H2 blocker + PPI combination (additive acid suppression not demonstrated)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Alginate-based formulations (Gaviscon Advance) as effective as PPI for nonerosive GERD in some RCTs; LINX magnetic sphincter augmentation device (FDA approved 2012, underused); dietary modification (low-acid, low-fat, eliminating triggers) as primary rather than adjunct
diseases.frontier_signal: Transoral incisionless fundoplication (TIF) procedure — no incision, outpatient, durable at 5 years; Vonoprazan (P-CAB) for PPI-refractory cases; microbiome-targeted approaches for functional heartburn vs true GERD differentiation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous reversion to sinus rhythm occurs in ~50% of paroxysmal AF within 24-48 hours; persistent AF almost never self-terminates; rate-control (the dominant strategy) by definition keeps the patient in AF
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Catheter ablation achieves freedom from AF in ~70-80% at 1 year (paroxysmal), 50-60% (persistent); ablation superior to antiarrhythmic drugs (52% vs 71% AF recurrence, CABANA trial); EAST-AFNET 4 showed early rhythm control reduced major adverse CV events vs rate control
diseases.gap_size: Large — rate control vs rhythm control debate has shifted but practice hasn't caught up
diseases.barrier_detail: Guidelines now support earlier rhythm control and ablation, but rate control with lifelong anticoagulation remains the dominant practice pattern; AF management is described as 'moving ahead of established guidelines' per ESC 2023
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — ESC guidelines restructured with new AF management acronym (AF-CARE); recommendation for early rhythm control strengthened (EAST-AFNET 4 data); 2014 ACC/AHA rate-vs-rhythm neutral stance increasingly outdated; first-line rate control drugs (beta-blockers, digoxin) unchanged since 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Digoxin for rate control (increased mortality signal in observational data, narrow therapeutic window; still widely used); amiodarone as first-line antiarrhythmic in younger patients (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic toxicity; should be last-resort); rate control as default before rhythm assessment in younger patients
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Risk factor management as active AF treatment (weight loss, alcohol cessation, sleep apnea treatment — each reducing AF burden by 30-50%); cardiac rehabilitation post-ablation; omega-3 FA (STRENGTH and REDUCE-IT conflicting data)
diseases.frontier_signal: Pulsed field ablation (PFA) — non-thermal, cardiac-specific, reducing esophageal injury risk (CE marked 2021, FDA approved 2023); AI-guided ablation with real-time lesion assessment; gene therapy targeting connexin-43 remodeling

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Structural progression is essentially universal over 10+ years without intervention; pain fluctuates but functional decline is the expected trajectory; no disease-modifying therapy exists as of 2024
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: No cure or disease modification available; total joint replacement (TJR) achieves 85-90% patient satisfaction and near-elimination of pain but is not disease reversal; weight loss of >10% reduces pain scores ~50% and slows structural progression; exercise therapy comparable to NSAIDs for pain relief
diseases.gap_size: Large for pain management vs disease modification (disease modification doesn't exist); moderate for functional outcomes (exercise and weight loss underutilized vs medication)
diseases.barrier_detail: OARSI formally petitioned the FDA in 2016 to recognize OA as a serious disease with no cure and no disease-modifying interventions — confirmed in FDA's criteria. Practice gap: NSAIDs have been first-line for >100 years despite long-term evidence of joint damage acceleration and GI/CV risk; exercise consistently outperforms medication in functional outcomes but is rarely 'prescribed'
diseases.last_soc_change: 2019-2020 — OARSI/ACR guidelines formally de-emphasized acetaminophen (evidence of minimal efficacy) and added conditional recommendation against opioids; NSAIDs have been first-line since the 1960s; duloxetine added ~2010 as centrally-acting option
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term oral NSAID use (4-year MRI data shows NSAIDs may accelerate cartilage loss; GI bleed and CV risk substantial in elderly OA population); intra-articular corticosteroid injections repeated >3x/year (joint damage acceleration); opioids for chronic OA pain (mortality signal in observational data); acetaminophen (not meaningfully better than placebo in recent RCTs for OA pain)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections — multiple RCTs showing 3-6 month pain reduction; low-level laser therapy (LLLT); hydrogen sulfide donors (early animal data); senolytic therapy (dasatinib + quercetin — first human OA trial published 2019 showing reduced senescent cells)
diseases.frontier_signal: Lorecivivint (Wnt pathway inhibitor, Phase 3 failed 2022 but variant study ongoing); sprifermin (FGF-18, cartilage anabolic, Phase 2 positive); anti-nerve growth factor (tanezumab — FDA rejected over joint safety, remains contested); gene therapy (AAV-based IGF-1 delivery to joint)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive and irreversible by definition; MMSE decline 1.07-3.4 points/year untreated; no spontaneous remission
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) slow MMSE decline to 0.2-1.37 points/year — slowing, not stopping; lecanemab (anti-amyloid) reduced clinical decline rate by 27% at 18 months in early AD (CLARITY AD, 2022); donanemab showed 35% slowing in amyloid/tau positive group
diseases.gap_size: Small for symptom slowing; biological ceiling is genuinely low with current approaches
diseases.barrier_detail: True disease reversal or cure is not achievable with any current therapy; cholinesterase inhibitors have consistent but modest effects (average 2.7-point ADAS-Cog improvement vs placebo); anti-amyloid biologics represent a genuine mechanistic step forward but are not cures and have significant side effects (ARIA — amyloid-related imaging abnormalities in 21-36%)
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA full approval of lecanemab (Leqembi) for early AD — first disease-modifying therapy; 2003 memantine added; cholinesterase inhibitors approved 1993-2001; aducanumab (2021) withdrawn after controversy; this represents the first genuine SOC change since early 2000s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Vitamin E supplementation (early trials positive, replication failed; still widely used); ginkgo biloba (multiple large RCTs negative, including GuidAge 5-year trial); NSAIDs as preventive or therapeutic (consistently negative in trials); huperzine A (widespread but no Phase 3 evidence)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Metabolic/lifestyle intervention (FINGER, MAPT, PREDIVA trials — multimodal interventions show 30% reduction in cognitive decline); ketogenic diet (emerging mechanistic rationale and early trials); photobiomodulation for cognitive function (small RCTs, Phase 2 active); methylene blue (low-dose, Phase 2)
diseases.frontier_signal: Lecanemab and donanemab (first approved disease-modifying therapies); tau-targeting antibodies (semorinemab, zagotenemab Phase 3); GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide for AD, multiple Phase 3 including EVOKE trial); antisense oligonucleotides (APOE-targeting); ultrasound-mediated blood-brain barrier opening for drug delivery

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Hashimoto's (most common cause) is irreversible in majority; spontaneous recovery in <5%; subclinical hypothyroidism spontaneously normalizes in ~30-40% of cases within 2 years without treatment
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: T4 monotherapy normalizes TSH in nearly all patients and resolves symptoms in ~60%; up to 40% of T4-treated patients remain symptomatic despite normalized TSH; T4+T3 combination or desiccated thyroid extract (DTE) preferred by ~50% of patients in blinded preference trials
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — within the treated population, a large minority are inadequately treated by the standard-of-care T4 monotherapy approach
diseases.barrier_detail: Up to 40% of patients remain symptomatic on T4 despite guideline-defined adequate treatment; T3 combination and DTE are rarely offered in primary care despite guideline acknowledgment that a trial may be appropriate; TSH-only monitoring misses tissue-level hypothyroidism
diseases.last_soc_change: 2014 — ATA guidelines last updated; acknowledged T4/T3 combination as appropriate trial in symptomatic patients; 2019 ETA/BTA guidelines more explicitly acknowledged need for personalized approach; T4 monotherapy has been standard since 1980s displacement of DTE
diseases.outdated_treatments: TSH-only monitoring without free T4 and T3 measurement in symptomatic patients; rigid adherence to T4 monotherapy in symptomatic patients without considering combination or DTE; desiccated thyroid extract (DTE) was the only treatment for 110+ years before being discarded largely for convenience reasons rather than evidence of inferiority
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Selenium supplementation (reduces anti-TPO antibodies in Hashimoto's, may slow progression); low-dose naltrexone (LDN) in autoimmune thyroid disease; vitamin D optimization; gluten-free diet in Hashimoto's with coexistent celiac or non-celiac gluten sensitivity
diseases.frontier_signal: Thyroid regeneration via stem cells (early animal models); mRNA-based thyroid hormone delivery; immune tolerization for Hashimoto's prevention; thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor antibody (TRAb) targeted therapy for Graves' disease

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: ~90% of acute LBP episodes resolve within 6 weeks regardless of treatment; chronic LBP (>3 months) shows only 30-40% recovery at 1 year and high recurrence; opioid prescribing correlates inversely with recovery
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) + graded exercise: 50-70% sustained improvement at 1 year in chronic LBP; Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) — 66% pain-free or nearly pain-free at 1 year (JAMA Psychiatry 2021, vs 20% placebo); early active rehabilitation (vs bed rest) dramatically reduces chronification
diseases.gap_size: Large for psychologically-informed approaches vs current opioid/surgical dominant model
diseases.barrier_detail: Opioid prescribing for chronic LBP is associated with worse long-term outcomes yet remains extremely common; spinal fusion surgery rates 40x higher in US than UK despite no superiority over conservative care in most indications; active rehabilitation demonstrably superior to passive treatment but requires patient effort and provider time
diseases.last_soc_change: 2017 — ACP guidelines explicitly recommended against opioids as first-line for chronic LBP; 2016 CDC opioid prescribing guidelines; imaging-guided approach deprioritized; exercise and CBT as first-line since 2017 — but implementation is poor
diseases.outdated_treatments: Opioids as first-line or long-term treatment (CDC guidelines explicitly against this; long-term opioid therapy correlates with worse functional outcomes in LBP); routine lumbar spinal fusion for nonspecific chronic LBP (RCTs show no superiority over intensive rehabilitation); prolonged bed rest (explicitly contraindicated since 1990s, yet still advised by some practitioners); routine NSAID long-term use without reassessment
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Pain Reprocessing Therapy (PRT) — 66% near-pain-free at 1 year; mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) — RCT evidence; intradiscal biologics (platelet-rich plasma, stem cells — Phase 2 trials); spinal cord stimulation for failed back surgery syndrome
diseases.frontier_signal: Pain Reprocessing Therapy (growing RCT base); resiniferatoxin (ultra-potent TRPV1 agonist causing ablation of pain-signaling neurons — Phase 1/2); biologic disc regeneration (GDF-5, TGF-beta intradiscal injection Phase 2); dorsal root ganglion stimulation (FDA approved 2016 for CRPS, expanding to LBP)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Migraine frequency fluctuates; spontaneous remission (no attacks for 12+ months) occurs in ~25% over 10-year follow-up; 40% of migraine sufferers qualify for preventive therapy but only 3-13% receive it
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) achieve ≥50% reduction in monthly migraine days in 50-70% of patients; complete response (near-zero days) in 10-30%; acute triptans achieve 2-hour pain freedom in 30-40%
diseases.gap_size: Large — primarily an access/utilization gap for preventive therapy
diseases.barrier_detail: Only 3-13% of the ~10 million US adults who qualify for preventive therapy receive it; prior authorization barriers for CGRP mAbs are substantial; widespread underdiagnosis; opioid prescribing for migraine in ED associated with medication overuse headache
diseases.last_soc_change: 2018-2020 — FDA approved first CGRP-targeted preventive therapies (erenumab 2018, fremanezumab/galcanezumab 2018-2019, atogepant 2021); prior prevention (beta-blockers, topiramate, amitriptyline) unchanged since 1990s-2000s; triptans first-line acute since 1993
diseases.outdated_treatments: Opioids for acute migraine (associated with medication overuse headache, MOH, in 50-70% of frequent users; actually worsens long-term migraine frequency); opioids in ED for acute migraine (inferior to IV prochlorperazine + diphenhydramine, yet remain common); barbiturate-containing analgesics (Fioricet/butalbital — MOH risk, no superiority over NSAIDs)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Magnesium IV/oral (effective for acute migraine and prevention; dramatically underused); riboflavin 400mg/day (preventive; NNT ~3 in RCTs); CoQ10 300mg/day (Cochrane-reviewed); neuromodulation devices (Cefaly transcranial supraorbital stimulation, FDA cleared; gammaCore vagus nerve stimulation)
diseases.frontier_signal: Gepants (oral CGRP receptor antagonists — ubrogepant, rimegepant — acute and preventive dual role); lasmiditan (5-HT1F agonist without vasoconstriction, for cardiovascular-contraindicated patients); psilocybin for cluster headache (NIH-funded Phase 2 underway); transcranial magnetic stimulation (SpringTMS, FDA cleared)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Untreated relapsing MS: ~1 relapse/year; disability accumulates over time in ~85% who convert to secondary progressive MS; spontaneous remission from MS is not possible
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: High-efficacy DMTs (natalizumab, alemtuzumab, ocrelizumab, cladribine) achieve 0 clinical relapses in 50-70% at 2 years and >95% MRI lesion suppression; early high-efficacy treatment shows superior outcomes vs platform therapy escalation
diseases.gap_size: Large — massive gap between platform DMT (interferons, glatiramer) and high-efficacy DMT outcomes, but practice still favors escalation model
diseases.barrier_detail: Treat-to-target with early high-efficacy therapy is superior to the traditional safety-first escalation model (TREAT-MS, DELIVER-MS trials) but most MS neurologists still start with platform therapies; hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) achieves 70-80% no-evidence-of-disease-activity (NEDA) at 5 years but is rarely offered
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved tolebrutinib and fenebrutinib (BTK inhibitors) for primary progressive MS; ocrelizumab approved for PPMS 2017 (first ever treatment); platform therapies (interferons, glatiramer) unchanged since 1993-1996; paradigm shift to high-efficacy first gaining guideline endorsement only now
diseases.outdated_treatments: Escalation approach starting with interferon-beta or glatiramer acetate despite evidence that early high-efficacy treatment substantially reduces long-term disability accumulation; repeated IV corticosteroid use for frequent relapses without reassessing disease-modifying therapy adequacy
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Biotin (high-dose, 100-300mg/day) in progressive MS — Phase 3 equivocal but ongoing; vitamin D optimization (strong epidemiological association, RCT evidence modest); intermittent fasting/ketogenic diet for neuroprotection (animal models; Phase 2 underway); exercise as neuroprotective intervention
diseases.frontier_signal: BTK inhibitors (tolebrutinib, fenebrutinib, evobrutinib) for progressive MS — target CNS-resident microglia; remyelination strategies (opicinumab, PIPE-307); HSCT (MIST trial showed superiority over mitoxantrone; Phase 3 vs alemtuzumab underway); CAR-T cell therapies in early trials

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: MASH (formerly NASH) with fibrosis progresses to cirrhosis in ~20% over 10 years on standard care (which until 2024 was lifestyle modification only); fibrosis regression occurs spontaneously in ~25% over 5 years with sustained weight loss
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Resmetirom (Rezdiffra) — first FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for MASH (March 2024): 26-30% histological resolution at 52 weeks vs 10% placebo; bariatric surgery achieves MASH resolution in 85% and fibrosis regression in 35% at 5 years; >7-10% weight loss resolves MASH in ~50%
diseases.gap_size: Large — first drug only just approved in 2024 after decades of failed trials; practice still dominated by lifestyle advice alone
diseases.barrier_detail: Until March 2024, there was literally no approved pharmacotherapy for MASH despite it being a leading cause of cirrhosis and liver transplant; the field repeatedly failed Phase 3 (obeticholic acid, elafibranor, selonsertib, cenicriviroc — all negative); resmetirom is a genuine breakthrough but access and prescribing uptake are still ramping up
diseases.last_soc_change: March 2024 — resmetirom (Rezdiffra) FDA approved — the first pharmacotherapy for MASH; prior standard was weight loss and lifestyle only since MASH was described in 1980
diseases.outdated_treatments: Vitamin E (early RCT positive in non-diabetic NASH — PIVENS trial; subsequent use became widespread but long-term safety concerns emerged; not guideline-recommended in diabetics or cirrhosis); metformin (repeatedly negative in MASH RCTs despite glucose benefit); omega-3 fatty acids at standard doses (Phase 3 negative for histology despite lipid benefit)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Coffee consumption (3+ cups/day associated with 40% reduced fibrosis progression — not a supplement, but a genuine finding); prebiotic/probiotic interventions; SGLT2 inhibitors (secondary benefit on liver histology in MASH — RCT data emerging); exercise as primary therapy (reduces liver fat independently of weight loss)
diseases.frontier_signal: Resmetirom (just approved); semaglutide for MASH (Phase 3 ESSENCE trial — histological resolution 63% vs 34%; results 2024); lanifibranor (PPAR-alpha/delta/gamma, Phase 3 NATIVE trial positive); combination approaches; FGF21 analogs (pegbelfermin); FXR agonists next generation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: GFR decline averages 2-5 mL/min/year on standard care (ACE/ARB + blood pressure control); progression to end-stage renal disease (ESRD) in ~20-30% at 10 years for moderate CKD; reversal essentially impossible once structural fibrosis established
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce progression to ESRD by 30-35% in CKD trials (EMPA-KIDNEY, DAPA-CKD); finerenone (non-steroidal MRA) adds 15-20% additional protection; combination slows but does not reverse disease
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — SGLT2 inhibitors represent a genuine paradigm shift but are dramatically underused
diseases.barrier_detail: SGLT2 inhibitors have robust evidence for CKD protection regardless of diabetes status (EMPA-KIDNEY 2023) but prescribing rates in non-diabetic CKD remain very low; ACE/ARB as sole strategy (established in 1990s-2000s) still dominant despite the new data
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — KDIGO guidelines updated to include SGLT2 inhibitors as first-line in CKD regardless of diabetes (EMPA-KIDNEY data); finerenone added for CKD in T2DM; ACE/ARB first-line unchanged since 2000; this is the first major CKD pharmacotherapy advance in 20 years
diseases.outdated_treatments: ACE/ARB monotherapy without SGLT2 inhibitor in eligible CKD patients (KDIGO now recommends SGLT2 inhibitor as co-first-line); protein restriction beyond moderate levels (evidence equivocal, high compliance burden); erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) to high hemoglobin targets (increased CV risk — TREAT and CHOIR trials; target now 10-11 g/dL, not 13+)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: SGLT2 inhibitors (just becoming standard, but still underused); low-protein plant-predominant diet (PLADO trial — reduces hyperfiltration, lower acid load); bicarbonate supplementation (slows GFR decline in acidotic CKD — small but real effect); bardoxolone methyl (NRF2 activator, Phase 3 CARDINAL trial positive for CKD in Alport syndrome)
diseases.frontier_signal: Sparsentan (dual endothelin/angiotensin antagonist — FDA approved for IgA nephropathy 2023); avacopan (complement inhibitor for ANCA vasculitis CKD); atrasentan (selective endothelin-A, Phase 3 ZENITH trial positive for IgA nephropathy 2024); kidney organoid-based replacement therapy (5-10 year horizon)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Essentially zero for established T1DM (honeymoon phase occurs in 50-60% after diagnosis but lasts 3-12 months then ends); the disease is permanent autoimmune destruction of beta cells
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Closed-loop insulin delivery (artificial pancreas systems — Control-IQ, Omnipod 5) achieves 70-80% time-in-range vs 60% with standard therapy; teplizumab (anti-CD3 mAb) delays T1DM onset by 2+ years in at-risk individuals; islet cell transplantation achieves insulin independence in ~44% at 5 years (CITR data)
diseases.gap_size: Moderate-large — closed-loop approaches are increasingly standard but true insulin independence is rare with any available therapy
diseases.barrier_detail: Beta cell regeneration and transplantation face immune rejection barriers (all islet recipients require chronic immunosuppression); closed-loop systems dramatically improve quality of life and glycemic control but require intensive engagement and are expensive; teplizumab (FDA approved 2022) for delay is not a cure
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — teplizumab (Tzield) FDA approved for Stage 2 T1DM (first immunotherapy to delay progression); 2023 closed-loop systems widely recommended; 2021 Omnipod 5 cleared; ADA continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) recommended for all T1DM 2020; insulin unchanged as backbone since 1921
diseases.outdated_treatments: Multiple daily injection (MDI) with finger-stick monitoring in patients who would benefit from CGM + closed-loop (evidence clearly superior but access barriers remain); fixed insulin regimens without carbohydrate counting; NPH insulin use in settings where insulin analogues are available
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: SGLT2 inhibitors as adjunct in T1DM (off-label, DKA risk with protocol; FDA rejected for T1D but widely used with mitigation strategies); GLP-1 agonists as adjunct (weight and dose reduction); ketogenic diet (very low-carb significantly reduces insulin requirements and variability)
diseases.frontier_signal: Vertex VX-880 (stem-cell derived islet cells — Phase 1/2, insulin independence achieved in treated patients 2023); teplizumab for prevention in at-risk individuals; anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) + GCSF for beta cell preservation; encapsulated islet transplantation (no immunosuppression needed — Phase 1 underway)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Annual mortality ~22% on standard care; only 5-11% of HFrEF patients achieve normalisation of ejection fraction (HFrecEF) with GDMT; HFpEF has no disease-modifying therapy
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: GDMT (ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i) reduces CV mortality by 38% and hospitalization by 25% vs historical standard; reverse cardiac remodelling (EF improvement >10%) in 40-50% of HFrEF with full quadruple therapy; cardiac resynchronisation therapy (CRT) improves EF in 70% of LBBB patients
diseases.gap_size: Large — quadruple GDMT dramatically underused; <25% of eligible HFrEF patients receive all four pillars
diseases.barrier_detail: Full quadruple therapy guideline-endorsed since 2021 but implementation is poor; SGLT2i in HF still widely under-prescribed despite Class 1A recommendation; ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) uptake slow due to cost
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — ESC/ACC/AHA guidelines added SGLT2i as fourth pillar (EMPEROR-Reduced, DAPA-HF); ARNI as backbone replacing ACE/ARB since 2016 (PARADIGM-HF); beta-blocker/MRA foundation unchanged since 1999-2003
diseases.outdated_treatments: ACE inhibitor without upgrading to ARNI in eligible patients; digoxin as routine add-on (no mortality benefit, toxicity risk; still widely used in AF + HF); high-dose diuretics without volume optimisation guided by invasive monitoring or bioimpedance
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Cardiac rehab (30% mortality reduction, under-referred); remote haemodynamic monitoring (CardioMEMS PA sensor reduces HF hospitalisations 28%); iron replacement IV (AFFIRM-AHF: reduces HF hospitalisation); dietary sodium restriction (evidence debated — SODIUM-HF trial null)
diseases.frontier_signal: Mavacamten (myosin inhibitor, FDA approved 2022 for HCM; trials in HFpEF); omecamtiv mecarbil (cardiac myosin activator for HFrEF — Phase 3 GALACTIC-HF marginal benefit); gene therapy (AAV9-SERCA2a — Phase 2 AGENT-HF); cardiac organoid-based tissue therapy

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous PASI90 remission essentially zero without treatment; natural fluctuation occurs but complete clearance rare; <5% achieve sustained spontaneous remission over 5 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: IL-17/IL-23 biologics achieve PASI100 (complete skin clearance) in 40-87% depending on agent and timeframe; complete clearance is now a realistic treatment goal, not a rarity
diseases.gap_size: Large — most patients never reach biologic therapy despite clear eligibility; on-drug clearance rates are exceptional but drug-free remission after cessation is short (12-32 weeks median for PASI loss)
diseases.barrier_detail: Most moderate-severe psoriasis patients remain on topicals or older systemics (methotrexate, cyclosporine) for years before biologics are offered; prior authorisation barriers significant; psoriasis is associated with cardiovascular risk requiring systemic treatment beyond skin clearance
diseases.last_soc_change: 2020-2022 — IL-23 inhibitors (risankizumab 2019, guselkumab 2017) established complete clearance as the new standard goal; IL-17 inhibitors since 2015; prior biologics (anti-TNF, ustekinumab) since 2009; methotrexate first-line unchanged since 1960s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term cyclosporine (nephrotoxicity limits to 1-2 year courses; still used as bridge); PUVA (psoralen + UVA phototherapy — carcinogenic with cumulative exposure; largely replaced by NB-UVB but still prescribed); coal tar preparations (low efficacy, cosmetically problematic, still in OTC products)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) — case reports and small series in psoriasis; omega-3 supplementation (reduces PASI in trials); Mediterranean diet (reduces systemic inflammation); topical cannabidiol (early RCT data); stress reduction interventions (psoriasis is stress-responsive)
diseases.frontier_signal: Oral IL-23 inhibitors (JNJ-77242113, Phase 2 ANTHEM-PsO); deucravacitinib (TYK2 inhibitor — FDA approved 2022, oral, no black box); bimekizumab (dual IL-17A/F inhibitor — FDA approved 2023); spesolimab (IL-36 receptor inhibitor for GPP — approved 2022)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive neurodegeneration — no spontaneous remission; untreated motor decline averages 3-5 UPDRS points/year; non-motor symptoms (depression, cognitive decline, autonomic dysfunction) also progressive
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Levodopa/carbidopa: 40-50% motor improvement in early disease but efficacy wanes (motor fluctuations in 50% at 5 years, dyskinesias in 30%); deep brain stimulation (DBS): 50-60% reduction in off-time, 60-70% reduction in dyskinesias at 5 years; LCIG (levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel infusion): near-continuous on-time in advanced patients
diseases.gap_size: Large — DBS significantly underused; no disease-modifying therapy exists; current treatments are purely symptomatic
diseases.barrier_detail: No neuroprotective or disease-modifying treatment is approved despite decades of research; DBS is offered too late in disease course (often 10+ years after diagnosis vs evidence for benefit in earlier disease — EARLYSTIM trial); focused ultrasound (FDA cleared 2021) underused as non-invasive alternative
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA cleared focused ultrasound thalamotomy (FUS) for bilateral PD tremor; 2021 FDA cleared Parkinson's-specific wearable (KinetiGraph); levodopa has been the backbone since 1967 — one of the longest-unchanged standards in medicine
diseases.outdated_treatments: Anticholinergic drugs (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl) for tremor in older patients (severe cognitive and urinary side effects; inappropriate in >65 years yet still prescribed); amantadine at standard doses for dyskinesia before considering DBS; MAO-B inhibitors without reassessment of levodopa optimisation
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (SPARX and CYCLE trials: high-intensity exercise slows motor progression; potentially neuroprotective); focused ultrasound (FUS) thalamotomy; dietary: gut-brain axis interventions (prebiotics, probiotics — emerging Phase 2 data); photobiomodulation for neuroprotection (early trials)
diseases.frontier_signal: Alpha-synuclein immunotherapy (prasinezumab, PASADENA/PADOVA trials — Phase 2 equivocal for motor, positive on certain imaging biomarkers); GLP-1 agonists (lixisenatide PPMI Phase 2 — significant motor benefit signal 2024); gene therapy (AAV2-AADC for advanced PD; AAV-GDNF Phase 2); stem cell-derived dopaminergic neurons (Aspen Neuroscience, BlueRock — Phase 1/2)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous elite control (natural viremic suppression without ART) in <1% of people living with HIV; untreated, median time to AIDS ~10 years; viral rebound within weeks of ART interruption in 99%+ of patients
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Modern ART (integrase inhibitor-based): viral suppression to undetectable in >95% within 6 months, maintained indefinitely on treatment; life expectancy near-normalised with early treatment; 7 documented sterilising cures worldwide (all via CCR5-Δ32 stem cell transplantation); post-treatment controllers (VISCONTI cohort): ~5-15% of early-treated individuals achieve durable ART-free viremic suppression
diseases.gap_size: Very large (sterilising cure); near-zero biological gap for quality-of-life control on ART; large access gap globally
diseases.barrier_type: biological (sterilising cure) + access (ART, particularly in LMIC)
diseases.barrier_detail: ART is the most successful chronic disease management story in medicine but is definitionally not a cure; 39 million people living with HIV globally, only 77% on ART, only 72% virally suppressed (2023 UNAIDS); broadly neutralising antibodies (bNAbs) showing promising functional remission signals (ACTG A5386, 2025)
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022-2023 — long-acting injectables (cabotegravir + rilpivirine every 2 months, lenacapavir every 6 months) transformed adherence paradigm; dolutegravir-based regimens as first-line since 2018; ART inception for all regardless of CD4 count since 2015 (START trial); first drug (AZT) 1987
diseases.outdated_treatments: Older NRTIs (stavudine, didanosine) still used in some LMIC settings despite lipoatrophy and neuropathy; CD4-gated treatment initiation (replaced by treat-all since 2015); unboosted PI regimens (higher pill burden, metabolic effects vs INSTIs)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Long-acting PrEP (lenacapavir 6-monthly injection, PURPOSE 1/2 trials: 100% efficacy in PURPOSE 1 for adolescent girls); broadly neutralising antibodies (bNAbs) VRC01, 3BNC117 for functional remission; latency-reversing agents combined with immune checkpoint blockade
diseases.frontier_signal: bNAb combinations (ACTG A5386 — functional remission signal 2025); lenacapavir + bNAb for HIV cure; gene editing (zinc finger nucleases, CRISPR targeting CCR5 and HIV reservoir — EBT-101 Phase 1/2); stem cell-derived CCR5-Δ32 gene editing (City of Hope trial)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous early neurological improvement in 10-20% within 24 hours of ischaemic stroke without intervention; functional independence at 3 months in only 40-50% without revascularisation; 15% 30-day mortality untreated
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: IV thrombolysis (tPA/tenecteplase) within 4.5 hours: increases odds of favourable outcome by 30%; thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion within 24 hours: 46% functional independence vs 27% control (DAWN/DEFUSE); combined thrombolysis + thrombectomy: best outcomes in eligible patients
diseases.gap_size: Very large — enormous treatment gap; only 2-15% of eligible patients receive thrombolysis; <5% receive thrombectomy worldwide
diseases.barrier_detail: The 4.5-hour and 24-hour time windows are frequently missed due to delayed recognition, delayed presentation, or lack of stroke centre access; in-hospital treatment delays add further losses; secondary prevention (antihypertensives, statins, anticoagulation for AF) chronically underprescribed post-stroke
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — tenecteplase replacing tPA as preferred thrombolytic; 2024 extended thrombectomy window up to 24 hours (DAWN, DEFUSE 3); 2019 ARISE II positive for mobile stroke units; tPA established 1996 (unchanged 27 years as the backbone); thrombectomy evidence since 2015
diseases.outdated_treatments: Neuroprotective agents (multiple agents failed in trials: NXY-059, citicoline, magnesium); haemodilution therapy; routine heparin anticoagulation in acute ischaemic stroke (harmful, no benefit — yet still used); dexamethasone for cerebral oedema in ischaemic stroke (evidence-free)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Mobile stroke units (MSU) reduce time-to-treatment by 18-22 minutes; intensive physiotherapy protocols (VECTORS trial); TMS for motor recovery post-stroke; vagus nerve stimulation (MicroTransponder Vivistim — FDA cleared 2021 for stroke recovery); stem cell therapy (SB623 Phase 2, multicentre)
diseases.frontier_signal: Nerinetide (neuroprotective peptide — ESCAPE-NA1 trial showed benefit in combination with thrombectomy); tenecteplase superiority over tPA (TASTE-A, EXTEND-IA TNK); AI-guided stroke triage tools (already reducing time-to-treatment); gene therapy for vascular malformations

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous regression is rare (<0.5% of cases); 5-year survival without treatment: <5% for advanced NSCLC; ~20% for stage I-II with surgery
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: NSCLC with driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, MET, KRAS G12C): targeted therapy achieves 70-90% response rates; PFS 16-19 months with osimertinib (FLAURA2 combination up to 25 months); immunotherapy (pembrolizumab): 5-year OS ~31% in high PD-L1 expressers vs ~16% chemotherapy; SBRT for early-stage inoperable: 80-90% local control at 3 years
diseases.gap_size: Very large between targeted/immunotherapy eligible and chemotherapy-only; large between treated and screened populations
diseases.barrier_detail: Biomarker testing (PD-L1, EGFR/ALK/ROS1 mutations) still not universal; only 5-6% of high-risk smokers undergo low-dose CT screening despite 20% mortality reduction (NLST trial); smoking cessation dramatically reduces incidence but is underfunded
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — osimertinib adjuvant ADAURA (Stage IB-IIIA EGFR+ NSCLC) standard of care; neoadjuvant chemo-immunotherapy (CheckMate 816) plus surgery now standard for resectable stage III; PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors first-line since 2016-2018; targeted therapy since 2010 (erlotinib)
diseases.outdated_treatments: Empiric chemotherapy without biomarker testing (platinum-doublets as sole approach in biomarker-unknown patients); thoracic radiation without concurrent chemotherapy for locally advanced NSCLC; palliative care referral only at end-stage vs early concurrent palliative integration (NEJM 2010 landmark trial showing survival benefit with early palliative)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) for treatment monitoring and minimal residual disease detection; exercise prehabilitation pre-surgery (reduces postoperative complications 30-40%); pulmonary rehabilitation during treatment; mistletoe extract (Iscador) — Phase 3 data equivocal but widely used in European integrative oncology
diseases.frontier_signal: KRAS G12C inhibitors (sotorasib, adagrasib — FDA approved 2021-2022); bispecific antibodies; TROP2-directed ADCs (datopotamab deruxtecan); TIGIT checkpoint combinations (Phase 3 ongoing); mRNA therapeutic vaccines for NSCLC (mRNA-4157 + pembrolizumab, KEYNOTE-942)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Essentially zero; 5-year survival stage I: ~90% (resectable); stage IV: ~15% overall, ~20-30% with modern therapy and liver metastasis resection
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Stage I-III with surgery + adjuvant chemotherapy: 60-80% 5-year survival; MSI-H/dMMR CRC: pembrolizumab first-line achieves ~55% 5-year PFS vs ~16% with chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-177); complete tumour regression with neoadjuvant therapy in rectal cancer (watch-and-wait): 15-30% complete pathological response allowing organ preservation
diseases.gap_size: Large screening gap: only 60-68% of US adults up-to-date with CRC screening; large treatment gap for MSI-H biomarker testing
diseases.barrier_detail: Colonoscopy screening is dramatically underused despite proven 60-70% mortality reduction; stool-based tests (FIT, Cologuard) improving uptake but sensitivity/specificity inferior; biomarker testing for MSI/MMR status still not universal; palliative colostomy vs sphincter-preserving approaches vary widely by provider
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — pembrolizumab first-line for MSI-H/dMMR mCRC standard of care (KEYNOTE-177); LONSURF (trifluridine/tipiracil) and fruquintinib for refractory disease; bevacizumab + FOLFOX/FOLFIRI backbone since 2004 essentially unchanged; EGFR inhibitors (cetuximab) in RAS wild-type since 2012
diseases.outdated_treatments: 5-FU alone without leucovorin or oxaliplatin in eligible patients; clinical follow-up without CEA monitoring and CT surveillance post-resection; liver metastasis deemed unresectable without multidisciplinary surgical review (resectability borderline — many patients excluded who could benefit from downsizing with chemotherapy)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (reduces CRC recurrence risk 25-35% in observational data; CHALLENGE trial underway); aspirin as adjuvant for PIK3CA-mutant CRC (BANDOC trial); dietary: high-fibre, low-red meat dietary patterns post-diagnosis; probiotic microbiome optimisation during chemotherapy
diseases.frontier_signal: Watch-and-wait after complete response in rectal cancer (organ preservation); ctDNA-guided adjuvant therapy (CIRCULATE-Japan, DYNAMIC-III); tumour-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy for MSS CRC; mRNA vaccines (mRNA-5671 targeting KRAS mutations, Phase 1)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous regression documented but rare (<0.5%); 5-year survival stage I: ~99%; stage IV: ~28% overall, improving with modern therapy
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: HER2+ metastatic: pertuzumab + trastuzumab + docetaxel (CLEOPATRA: 57.1-month median OS vs 40.8 months); HR+/HER2- with CDK4/6 inhibitors: 63-month PFS in first-line (MONALEESA-2); TNBC with pembrolizumab + chemotherapy: 23-month OS vs 16.1 months; pCR with neoadjuvant: 80-90% in HER2+ with dual HER2 blockade
diseases.gap_size: Varies greatly by subtype; HER2+ and HR+ have dramatically improved outcomes; TNBC lags; metastatic remains incurable in most patients
diseases.barrier_detail: Racial disparities in breast cancer mortality persist (Black women 40% higher mortality despite similar incidence); access to genetic testing (BRCA1/2) and CDK4/6 inhibitors varies significantly; germline testing vastly underutilised
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) transforming TNBC (sacituzumab govitecan 2020, trastuzumab deruxtecan for HER2-low 2022); olaparib adjuvant in BRCA-mutated HER2- (OlympiA 2021); CDK4/6 inhibitors first-line HR+/HER2- since 2015; trastuzumab HER2+ since 1998
diseases.outdated_treatments: Dose-dense chemotherapy in all settings (benefit only in specific subtypes); empiric chemotherapy without biomarker-guided selection in HR+/HER2- disease (CDK4/6 inhibitors now preferred); total mastectomy without breast conservation discussion for eligible patients (survival equivalent); routine bone scan/CT staging for early-stage (low yield, guideline-discordant)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (25-40% reduction in breast cancer mortality post-diagnosis); vitamin D optimisation; dietary fat reduction (WINS trial: 24% reduction in relapse in ER-negative); natriuretic peptide monitoring for cardioprotection during anthracycline therapy; yoga/mind-body practices for QoL and immune function
diseases.frontier_signal: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) for HER2-low (a new subtype essentially created by this drug); sacituzumab govitecan in earlier lines; BRCA-targeted PARP inhibitors expanding to earlier stages; personalised mRNA vaccines (mRNA-4157 in TNBC — Phase 2 data 2023); antibody-drug conjugate combinations

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Sustained weight loss of >5% without intervention: ~5-10% at 2 years; weight regain almost universal after behavioural intervention alone (95% return to within 5% of starting weight at 5 years on lifestyle alone)
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide 2.4mg): 15-17% mean weight loss at 68 weeks (STEP 1); tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1): 20-22% at 72 weeks (SURMOUNT-1); bariatric surgery: 25-35% sustained weight loss at 10 years with metabolic syndrome resolution
diseases.gap_size: Very large — effective pharmacotherapy now exists but is massively under-prescribed; access is the primary barrier
diseases.barrier_detail: Obesity is still widely treated as a lifestyle choice rather than a chronic disease by payers; GLP-1 medications (semaglutide, tirzepatide) cost $1,000-1,300/month and are excluded from most Medicare coverage; stigma in clinical settings reduces treatment-seeking; bariatric surgery used in <1% of eligible patients
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — tirzepatide (Zepbound) FDA approved for obesity; 2021 semaglutide (Wegovy) approved; 2024 semaglutide approved for CV risk reduction in obesity without diabetes (SELECT trial: 20% CV event reduction); prior obesity medications (orlistat, phentermine) essentially unchanged since 1990s-2000s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Phentermine monotherapy (approved 1959, short-term only; often used long-term off-label without evidence); appetite suppressants (fenfluramine/phen-fen — withdrawn 1997 for valvulopathy; some of same class derivatives re-emerging with different risk profiles); 'diet and exercise counselling' alone without pharmacotherapy or surgical referral in severe obesity
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Time-restricted eating (TRE) — independent of caloric restriction; resistance training (preserves muscle during GLP-1 induced weight loss); cagrilintide (amylin analogue) combination with semaglutide (CagriSema — Phase 3: 22.7% weight loss); endoscopic sleeve gastroplasty
diseases.frontier_signal: Amycretin (GLP-1/amylin co-agonist — Phase 2: 22% weight loss at 36 weeks); retatrutide (triple GIP/GLP-1/glucagon — Phase 2: 24.2% at 48 weeks, most effective agent to date); oral semaglutide for obesity (Phase 3 underway); bimagrumab (anti-ActRII — recomposes body without total weight loss)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Bone density decline of 1-3%/year in postmenopausal women without treatment; fracture risk increases exponentially with T-score decline; no spontaneous remission
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Bisphosphonates: 40-50% reduction in vertebral fractures, 20-40% hip fracture reduction; romosozumab (anti-sclerostin): 73% vertebral fracture reduction at 12 months (ARCH trial); abaloparatide/teriparatide (anabolic): BMD increase 9-12% at lumbar spine over 18 months
diseases.gap_size: Very large — post-fracture care gap is stark: only 20-30% of patients with fragility fracture receive osteoporosis treatment within 1 year
diseases.barrier_detail: The 'post-fracture care gap' is one of the most documented practice failures in medicine; the majority of fragility fracture patients leave hospital without osteoporosis diagnosis or treatment; fracture liaison services (FLS) fix this but are not universally implemented; anabolic therapies (romosozumab, teriparatide) drastically underused vs anti-resorptives
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — NOF/ASBMR guidelines recommend anabolic therapy first-line in very high-risk patients (T-score <-3.0 or prior hip/vertebral fracture); romosozumab approved 2019; 2016 GIOP guidelines updated; bisphosphonates standard since mid-1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Calcitonin nasal spray (evidence weak, potential cancer signal; largely abandoned in guidelines but still used); HRT as primary osteoporosis therapy without fracture risk stratification; calcium supplements >1000mg/day without dietary assessment (possible CV risk signal from supplemental calcium alone)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Resistance and weight-bearing exercise (osteogenic loading — high-impact stimuli); vibration therapy (low-magnitude mechanical signals — some trial data); dietary protein optimisation; fracture liaison services (FLS) achieving 40-50% secondary fracture reduction
diseases.frontier_signal: Setrusumab (anti-sclerostin for OI and osteoporosis — Phase 3); denosumab 6-monthly subcutaneous (FREEDOM trial); sequential anabolic-antiresorptive therapy protocols optimisation; AI-based FRAX risk stratification tools

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Natural episodic pattern: untreated, median episode length 3-6 months; recurrence in 90% within 5 years; functional impairment between episodes in 30-60%; suicide risk 20-30x general population
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Lithium reduces recurrence risk by 30-40% and suicide risk by 60-70% (unique anti-suicidal effect not shared by other mood stabilisers); combination pharmacotherapy + structured psychotherapy (IPSRT, CBT): 60-70% euthymia maintenance at 2 years in first-episode BD
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — lithium underuse is a genuine gap; psychotherapy chronically under-integrated with pharmacotherapy
diseases.barrier_detail: Lithium is the most evidence-based mood stabiliser with the only demonstrated anti-suicidal effect but is declining in use relative to newer anticonvulsants and antipsychotics partly due to monitoring requirements; many clinicians avoid lithium due to perceived complexity despite its superior evidence base
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — NICE/APA guidelines reaffirm lithium as first-line for BD prevention; 2019 cariprazine and lumateperone approved; atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, aripiprazole) as mood stabilisers since 2000s; lithium first used 1949
diseases.outdated_treatments: Valproate monotherapy in women of childbearing age (teratogenic, superior alternatives available; EU restricted 2018, compliance poor); antidepressant monotherapy in BD (high switch-to-mania risk; not guideline-recommended but frequently prescribed in unrecognised BD); long-term benzodiazepine co-administration
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — RCTs showing depressive symptom reduction; omega-3 fatty acids (adjunctive depressive symptom benefit); ketamine/esketamine for acute bipolar depression (off-label, growing evidence); circadian rhythm interventions (social rhythm therapy, light therapy for bipolar depression)
diseases.frontier_signal: Agonist-antagonist combination (lurasidone + lithium optimization protocols); personalized pharmacogenomics-guided lithium dosing; psilocybin for bipolar depression (Phase 2 underway with careful mania-risk monitoring); digital phenotyping for mood prediction and early intervention

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous full remission rare (<10%); after first episode, ~80% relapse within 5 years without maintenance antipsychotic; functional disability accumulates over time; 20% achieve good long-term outcome
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Long-acting injectable antipsychotics (LAIs): 30-50% reduction in relapse vs oral antipsychotics (PALIPERIDONE PALMITATE, aripiprazole monthly); clozapine for treatment-resistant: 30-60% response in patients who failed 2 other antipsychotics; critical period intensive early intervention (NAVIGATE program): 3x better employment, 30% less symptom burden at 2 years
diseases.gap_size: Large — LAIs chronically underused; clozapine for TRS grossly underused; early intervention services reach only a fraction of FEP patients
diseases.barrier_detail: Clozapine is used in only 4% of TRS patients in the US despite clear efficacy (should be ~30%); LAIs offered to only 15-25% of patients despite superior relapse prevention vs oral; stigma and structural barriers delay first treatment by 1-2 years on average; many patients never receive coordinated specialty care
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved xanomeline-trospium (KarXT/Cobenfy) — first non-dopamine-blocking mechanism for schizophrenia in 70 years (muscarinic agonist); 2019 lumateperone approved; 2015 LAI aripiprazole monthly approved; chlorpromazine first antipsychotic 1952; D2 blockade paradigm essentially unchanged since then until 2023
diseases.outdated_treatments: Typical antipsychotics (haloperidol, chlorpromazine) as routine first-line in new-onset schizophrenia (higher EPS, tardive dyskinesia, metabolic effects vs atypicals); polypharmacy of multiple antipsychotics without clozapine trial first; routine depot injections without coordinated care model
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA) — positive Phase 3 data for first-episode psychosis prevention in ultra-high risk individuals; sarcosine (glycine transporter inhibitor) adjunct for negative symptoms; cognitive remediation therapy; transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) for negative/cognitive symptoms
diseases.frontier_signal: Xanomeline-trospium (Cobenfy — first muscarinic mechanism; reduces positive and negative symptoms); trace amine-associated receptor 1 (TAAR1) agonists (ulotaront — Phase 3; ralmitaront); glutamatergic modulation approaches; personalized medicine via pharmacogenomics (CYP2D6 testing for antipsychotic dosing)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous remission by adulthood: 30-70% symptom reduction (depending on definition); functionally impairing ADHD persists into adulthood in 50-65% of childhood cases; DSMIV criteria met in adulthood in ~50%
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Stimulant medications (methylphenidate, amphetamines): 60-80% response rate; MTA study: combined medication + behavioural treatment superior to either alone; behavioural interventions alone: 30-50% symptom reduction in children; non-stimulant (atomoxetine, viloxazine): 40-60% response
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — substantial underdiagnosis especially in girls/women and adults; treatment gaps exist but pharmacotherapy is highly effective when accessed
diseases.barrier_detail: Stimulant shortages (2022-present in US) have left many patients without medication; adult ADHD massively underdiagnosed for decades; medication diversion concerns create barriers to legitimate access; non-pharmacological approaches (CBT, exercise, dietary interventions) chronically under-supported
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — viloxazine (Qelbree) extended-release approved for adults; 2021 serdexmethylphenidate (Azstarys) approved; 2019 lisdexamfetamine approved for adults and children; methylphenidate/amphetamine backbone essentially unchanged since 1955-1960
diseases.outdated_treatments: Pemoline (withdrawn 1999 for hepatotoxicity; residual use); routine ECG screening before stimulant initiation in children without cardiac history (not guideline-recommended but widely practiced); 'drug holidays' without individualized assessment (may impair academic/occupational performance when erroneously applied)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (25-30% improvement in executive function); dietary interventions (omega-3, elimination of artificial colours in sensitive children); neurofeedback (modest effect, expensive — best for medication-intolerant patients); working memory training (Cogmed — evidence for generalisation debated); mindfulness-based interventions
diseases.frontier_signal: Dasotraline (DRD+SERT inhibitor — Phase 3 data; different mechanism from classic stimulants); centanafadine (triple reuptake inhibitor); digital therapeutics (EndeavorRx — FDA cleared 2020, gamified attention training); cognitive enhancers targeting nicotinic receptor pathways

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: 30-40% of people with AUD achieve sustained abstinence without formal treatment at 1 year (natural recovery); but functional impairment, liver damage, and mortality risk accumulate; relapse in 50-60% within 1 year even after treatment
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Naltrexone (COMBINE trial): 23% abstinence rate vs 14% placebo at 16 weeks; combined pharmacotherapy + CBT: best outcomes (~30-40% sustained abstinence at 1 year); acamprosate: reduces relapse risk 14% (meta-analysis); liver cirrhosis reversal with abstinence: fibrosis regression in 30-40% at 5 years
diseases.gap_size: Very large — vast majority of people with AUD never receive pharmacotherapy; <10% receive evidence-based treatment
diseases.barrier_detail: AUD is massively undertreated; only 5.7% of people with AUD in the US received any treatment in 2019 (SAMHSA); naltrexone and acamprosate are dramatically underprescribed (<1% of AUD patients); 12-step/abstinence framing excludes patients seeking harm reduction; primary care providers rarely screen or treat
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — GLP-1 agonists showing alcohol craving reduction (semaglutide reducing alcohol use in trials); 2006 acamprosate approved; naltrexone monthly injectable (Vivitrol) since 2006; naltrexone oral since 1994; disulfiram since 1951; essentially unchanged core pharmacotherapy for 30 years
diseases.outdated_treatments: Disulfiram as primary treatment (poor adherence; dangerous without supervised administration; not superior to other options in RCTs); mandatory abstinence-only treatment frameworks (evidence supports harm reduction and reduced-drinking goals as valid endpoints; abstinence-only excludes many who could benefit); routine benzodiazepine use for outpatient detoxification beyond 3-5 days
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide reducing alcohol craving in RCTs — STAR trial underway); psilocybin-assisted therapy for AUD (NYU trial: 83% reduction in drinking days at 8 months); ketamine-assisted therapy (KARE trial positive); low-dose naltrexone (LDN) for harm reduction model
diseases.frontier_signal: GLP-1 agonists for AUD (STAR trial, Phase 3); psilocybin (promising Phase 2, MAPS and Johns Hopkins trials); GABA-B agonist baclofen (gabapentin as alternative — off-label but widely used in harm reduction settings); orexin receptor antagonists for craving management

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: GAD: spontaneous remission in ~25-30% at 1 year without treatment; social anxiety: 36% remission at 12 years untreated; panic disorder: 50-65% spontaneous partial remission but relapse common; 40-60% remain symptomatic at 5 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: CBT (gold standard): 60-80% treatment response, 40-60% full remission at 12-24 weeks; combination CBT + SSRI superior to either alone in some disorders; exposure therapy for specific phobias: 90%+ response; SSRI/SNRI alone: 50-60% response
diseases.gap_size: Large — CBT is consistently superior to medication for long-term outcomes but is massively underused relative to pharmacotherapy
diseases.barrier_detail: SSRIs/SNRIs are first-line by default in most primary care settings; CBT is guideline-recommended but wait times of 6-18 months are common; digital CBT (iCBT) is evidence-based but rarely prescribed; benzodiazepines continue to be prescribed long-term despite dependence risk
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved brexanolone for postpartum anxiety/depression; 2022 buspirone repositioning; first-line SSRIs/SNRIs essentially unchanged since early 1990s; duloxetine for GAD added 2004
diseases.outdated_treatments: Benzodiazepine long-term prescribing for chronic anxiety (dependence, cognitive impairment, falls in elderly; guidelines recommend short-term only; yet 30-40% of long-term BZD users have anxiety disorders with chronic prescriptions); propranolol monotherapy for social anxiety; barbiturates (rare but still encountered in elderly patients on legacy prescriptions)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Mindfulness-based interventions (MBSR equivalent to CBT in meta-analysis); exercise (particularly aerobic — RCT evidence for GAD and panic disorder); magnesium glycinate supplementation (anxiolytic effects in mild-moderate anxiety; RCT data); kava kava (Cochrane review: superior to placebo, safety concerns with hepatotoxicity); breathing-based techniques (cyclic sighing — Nature Medicine 2023)
diseases.frontier_signal: MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD/social anxiety (FDA PDUFA date 2024 — initial rejection, resubmission); psilocybin for treatment-resistant anxiety (Phase 2 at Imperial College, Johns Hopkins); ketamine nasal spray for acute anxiety (Phase 2); CGRP antagonists for comorbid migraine-anxiety

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous recovery (without MOUD) in 20-30% of OUD patients over 10 years; but overdose mortality risk during active disorder is extreme (5-10 per 1000 person-years); relapse rate 90%+ in first year after discontinuing MOUD
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Buprenorphine maintenance: 50-70% retention in treatment at 1 year vs <20% for placebo; naltrexone extended-release (Vivitrol): comparable to buprenorphine when initiated; methadone: superior retention but restricted distribution; overdose mortality reduced 50-70% with any MOUD
diseases.gap_size: Very large — only 20% of people with OUD receive MOUD; massive unmet need
diseases.barrier_detail: US eliminated the DEA X-waiver requirement for buprenorphine prescribing in December 2022 (a major policy win) but prescribing rates have increased slowly; stigma remains; methadone access restricted to OTPs (clinics); harm reduction infrastructure (fentanyl test strips, naloxone) still insufficiently deployed; 80% of people with OUD receive no treatment
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — DEA X-waiver elimination fully implemented; 2022 federal legislation allowing take-home methadone expanded; 2021 FDA approved injectable buprenorphine (Sublocade) for once-monthly dosing; buprenorphine SL since 2002; methadone for OUD since 1964
diseases.outdated_treatments: Abstinence-only treatment (detoxification without MOUD initiation — associated with 5-10x increased overdose mortality risk in first month post-discharge; widely practiced in many US facilities); opioid agonist therapy tapering (MOUD is most effective as indefinite maintenance, not tapered); 28-day treatment programs without MOUD transition
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Naloxone co-prescribing with any opioid prescription (expanding); fentanyl test strips (harm reduction); supervised consumption sites (SCS) — operating in New York since 2021; injectable extended-release naltrexone (CAM2038); contingency management (voucher-based reinforcement — highly effective but implementation barriers)
diseases.frontier_signal: Long-acting buprenorphine implants (Probuphine — 6-month duration); buprenorphine-naltrexone combination approaches; naloxone higher-dose formulations (8mg nasal spray Kloxxado for fentanyl analog reversal); fentanyl vaccines in preclinical development; AI-based overdose prediction and prevention platforms

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: 60-70% of childhood-onset AD achieves spontaneous remission by adolescence; adult-onset AD is more persistent; moderate-severe adult AD: <15% spontaneous clearance at 5 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα): 51% IGA 0/1 (clear/almost clear) at 16 weeks in SOLO trials; tralokinumab (anti-IL-13): 38% clear/almost clear; baricitinib (JAK1/2i): 16% EASI-100 (complete clearance) at 16 weeks; upadacitinib (JAK1i): 28-40% EASI-100; near-complete clearance now achievable for most patients
diseases.gap_size: Large — most patients managed with topical steroids for decades without biologic assessment; biologics available since 2017 but reach only a fraction of eligible patients
diseases.barrier_detail: Dupilumab approved 2017 but still not first-line in most health systems due to cost ($36,000/year); step therapy requirements force patients through years of topical steroids and immunosuppressants first; racial disparities in specialist access and biologic prescribing
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022-2023 — JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, baricitinib) and tralokinumab added; lebrikizumab approved 2023; dupilumab since 2017; prior standard (topical steroids, tacrolimus, cyclosporine) essentially unchanged since 1980s-2000s
