diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term topical corticosteroid on sensitive skin areas (periocular, genital) without switching to non-steroidal alternatives; wet wrap therapy without concurrent assessment for systemic options; systemic corticosteroid use beyond acute flare control (rebound flare risk on tapering; still over-relied upon)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Microbiome restoration (S. epidermidis transplant — Phase 2 data for AD); proactive maintenance therapy (applying tacrolimus 2x/week to previously affected areas — reduces flare frequency 50%); bleach baths (reduce S. aureus colonisation, reduces flare severity); vitamin D supplementation in deficient patients
diseases.frontier_signal: IL-31 inhibitor (nemolizumab — FDA approved 2024 for AD and prurigo nodularis); CRTH2/DP2 antagonist (fevipiprant); OX40 pathway inhibitors (rocatinlimab Phase 3); IL-33 inhibitors (itepekimab for asthma expanding to AD); oral PDE4 inhibitors (difamilast — approved Japan; pending US)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Acute insomnia (<3 months): 50-60% resolve spontaneously; chronic insomnia: only 20-25% spontaneous remission at 1 year; persistent insomnia in 50-75% without treatment
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I): 70-80% response rate; 40-60% remission (normalised sleep); durable at 6-24 months post-treatment; superior to sleep medication in long-term outcomes; digital CBT-I (Sleepio, Somryst) equivalent efficacy to in-person
diseases.gap_size: Very large — CBT-I is guideline-recommended as first-line but almost universally unavailable; medication is default
diseases.barrier_detail: AASM and most sleep guidelines recommend CBT-I as first-line over medication for chronic insomnia; fewer than 1% of insomnia sufferers have access to a CBT-I trained therapist; digital CBT-I exists and works but is rarely prescribed; benzodiazepines and z-drugs continue to dominate prescribing despite guidelines against long-term use
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — FDA approved daridorexant (Quviviq — orexin receptor antagonist); 2019 lemborexant; 2014 suvorexant (first orexin antagonist); z-drugs (zopiclone, zolpidem) since 1990s; benzodiazepines for sleep since 1960s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term benzodiazepine prescribing for insomnia (STOPP criteria: inappropriate in elderly; falls, cognitive impairment, dependence; yet ~15% of elderly adults in US are on chronic BZD); OTC antihistamines for chronic insomnia (diphenhydramine — rapid tolerance, next-day cognitive impairment); trazodone at sedating doses (efficacy data for insomnia very limited but extremely common off-label)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Sleep restriction therapy as standalone (abbreviated CBT-I delivered in primary care — non-inferior to full CBT-I in RESTING trial); stimulus control instructions; bright light therapy for circadian phase disorders; magnesium glycinate (modest but reproducible improvement in sleep quality in trials); L-theanine + GABA combination
diseases.frontier_signal: Digital therapeutics (Somryst — FDA De Novo approved 2020, prescription digital CBT-I); orexin dual antagonists (daridorexant showing less rebound insomnia vs older agents); cannabinoids (CBD for REM sleep behaviour disorder — Phase 2); personalized chronobiology — light-based circadian recalibration protocols

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: PCOS is a lifelong endocrine disorder; complete spontaneous resolution rare; some phenotypic improvement with menopause; fertility returns in 50-70% with ovulation induction; metabolic features persist
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Weight loss of 5-10% restores ovulation in 55-90% of anovulatory PCOS women; metformin restores ovulation in 30-50%; letrozole (ovulation induction): 27% live birth rate per cycle (PPCOS II); lifestyle modification: 50-80% improvement in menstrual regularity
diseases.gap_size: Large — lifestyle modification and metformin are evidence-based first-line but most patients receive OCPs (treating symptoms only, not the underlying metabolic disorder)
diseases.barrier_detail: OCP (oral contraceptive pill) is most commonly prescribed for PCOS, treating symptoms (irregular periods, hirsutism) without addressing the underlying insulin resistance and metabolic risk; OCP does not treat infertility and may worsen insulin sensitivity; the PCOS field has lacked a clear pharmacotherapy standard
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — International PCOS Guideline updated; letrozole formally recommended over clomiphene for ovulation induction; metformin recommended as first-line metabolic treatment; GLP-1 agonists emerging as adjunct; spironolactone for hyperandrogenism; first guidelines were 2011 (Endocrine Society)
diseases.outdated_treatments: Clomiphene citrate as first-line ovulation induction (inferior to letrozole — NEJM 2014 head-to-head); OCP as sole long-term treatment without metabolic assessment; ovarian drilling (laparoscopic — rarely indicated with letrozole available)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Inositol supplementation (myo-inositol + D-chiro-inositol) — multiple RCTs showing improvement in menstrual regularity, ovulation, insulin sensitivity comparable to metformin in some studies; dietary interventions (low-glycaemic diet — reduces insulin, androgens); N-acetylcysteine (NAC) as alternative to metformin in some cohorts; berberine
diseases.frontier_signal: GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide significantly improves ovarian function, menstrual regularity, and fertility outcomes in PCOS — SPGP trial Phase 3); tirzepatide for PCOS metabolic phenotype; progesterone receptor modulators; gut microbiome modulation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive disease; spontaneous improvement rare; limb ischaemia worsens in 25-30% over 5 years; major adverse limb events (MALE) in 10-15%/year in advanced PAD; major amputation in 5-10% at 5 years without revascularisation
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Supervised exercise therapy (SET): equivalent to endovascular revascularisation for claudication at 6-12 months (CLEVER trial); revascularisation: 70-80% 1-year limb salvage; SGLT2 inhibitors + GLP-1 agonists reduce MALE by 15-20% (AMPLITUDE-O, EMPA-REG); rivaroxaban + aspirin (COMPASS trial): 46% reduction in MALE
diseases.gap_size: Very large — supervised exercise therapy is first-line in guidelines but almost no patient is referred; PAD remains vastly underdiagnosed
diseases.barrier_detail: PAD is underdiagnosed (ABI testing rarely performed in primary care); supervised exercise therapy is guideline Class IA recommendation but reimbursed and accessible for <5% of PAD patients; cilostazol (PDE3 inhibitor) improves walking but is contraindicated in heart failure (common comorbidity); PAD mortality often exceeds coronary disease mortality in this population
diseases.last_soc_change: 2024 — rivaroxaban + aspirin (COMPASS) now guideline-recommended; 2023 SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 agonists recognised for limb protection; 2022 VOYAGER-PAD (rivaroxaban post-revascularisation); supervised exercise therapy Class IA since 2016; cilostazol approved 1999
diseases.outdated_treatments: Pentoxifylline for claudication (inconsistent evidence, largely abandoned in guidelines but still prescribed); angioplasty-only without drug-eluting balloon or stenting in femoropopliteal disease (inferior restenosis rates vs DCB); routine surgical bypass as first-line without trial of medical therapy and SET
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Supervised exercise therapy (consistently under-referred); home-based exercise programs (digital alternatives showing comparable benefit); stem cell therapy (STAR and MATISSE trials — Phase 2 positive for CLI); therapeutic angiogenesis (gene therapy — ANGES NV1FGF negative, others ongoing)
diseases.frontier_signal: LPA-lowering with inclisiran/olpasiran for cardiovascular event reduction in PAD; ANGPTL3 inhibitors; stem cell-derived EPC therapy for CLI; sonic hedgehog gene therapy (Phase 2); nitric oxide-releasing biomaterials for arterial repair

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Not applicable — sepsis is an acute life-threatening syndrome; hospital mortality 15-25% for sepsis, 30-45% for septic shock with standard care; long-term complications (post-sepsis syndrome) in 50-70% of survivors
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Hour-1 Bundle (blood cultures + antibiotics + fluids + vasopressors): 30-40% mortality reduction vs delayed care; vasopressin as vasopressor adjunct reduces vasopressor requirements; corticosteroids in septic shock: 3-5% absolute mortality reduction (ADRENAL, APROCCHSS); no truly curative intervention beyond source control
diseases.gap_size: Large — compliance with Hour-1 Bundle varies enormously across hospitals; 3-6 hour bundle completion only in 50-70% of hospitals
diseases.barrier_detail: Sepsis recognition delay is the primary driver of mortality; time-to-antibiotics is the single best predictor of outcome (each hour delay increases mortality 4-7%); early goal-directed therapy (EGDT) as originally described was abandoned after ProCESS/ARISE/ProMISe trials showed no benefit of specific protocol over usual care, creating confusion about what 'appropriate' standard care is
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — Surviving Sepsis Campaign updated Hour-1 Bundle; 2020 lactate monitoring formalised; steroid guidance revised after ADRENAL and APROCCHSS; Drotrecogin alfa (Xigris) withdrawn 2011 after Phase 3 failure; treatment approach largely unchanged since surviving sepsis campaign 2004
diseases.outdated_treatments: Drotrecogin alfa (Xigris — withdrawn 2011); tight glycaemic control (110-140 target) — NICE-SUGAR showed harm, now permissive glycaemia); early goal-directed therapy as originally specified (CVP/ScvO2 protocol — abandoned after 2015 trials); routine fresh frozen plasma prophylactically without bleeding indication
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Thiamine supplementation (benefit in metabolic acidosis subgroup); vitamin C + hydrocortisone (LOVIT trial vitamin C alone null, CITRIS-ALI null; combination remains studied); IVIG (limited evidence, meta-analysis equivocal); IL-6 receptor antagonists for cytokine storm sepsis subphenotype
diseases.frontier_signal: Host-response phenotyping (SRS1 vs SRS2 — hyperinflammatory vs hyper-immunosuppressed — allows phenotype-targeted treatment; VENOMS trial); IL-7 for immunoparalysis subtype; GM-CSF for monocyte restoration; AI-based early sepsis prediction (Epic Sepsis Model — widely implemented, performance debated); bacteriophage therapy for MDR infections

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: No spontaneous remission — autoimmune injury continues as long as gluten is consumed; villous atrophy persists in 30-40% at 5 years even on gluten-free diet (GFD) due to cross-contamination
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Strict gluten-free diet: histological remission (Marsh 0-1) in 60-70% at 2 years; mucosal healing complete in only ~40% at 5 years; non-responsive celiac in 7-30% (defined as persistent symptoms/histology on strict GFD)
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — GFD is effective but imperfect; adherence is the primary barrier; and no pharmacotherapy exists for GFD non-responders
diseases.barrier_detail: The GFD is the only treatment and no pharmacotherapy is approved, leaving 7-30% of non-responsive/refractory patients without additional options; even 'strict' adherence fails to achieve mucosal healing in a large minority due to food labelling gaps and cross-contamination; quality of life impact of GFD is substantial; refractory celiac disease Type II (RCDII) has very poor prognosis
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FALCATA Phase 3 (larazotide, tight junction modulator) — failed primary endpoint; 2023 ATLAS Phase 2 (latiglutenase, gluten-degrading enzyme) — some histological benefit; GFD has been the only treatment since celiac disease was first described in 1950s; no pharmacotherapy approved to date
diseases.outdated_treatments: Elemental diets for refractory celiac (limited evidence, highly restrictive); prolonged corticosteroid use for refractory celiac before budesonide or RCDII workup (budesonide is preferable with less systemic effect); routine HLA typing for diagnosis (DQ2/DQ8 testing is most useful for ruling out celiac, not confirming it)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Latiglutenase (KumaMax/STAN1 — gluten-degrading proteases; Phase 2 positive for reducing gluten-induced histological damage); probiotics (preliminary evidence for modulating gluten immunogenicity); larazotide acetate (tight junction regulator — repeated Phase 2/3 attempts, endpoint failures)
diseases.frontier_signal: Nexvax2 (gluten peptide desensitisation vaccine — Phase 2 failed 2019 primary endpoint but proof-of-concept established); ZED-1227 (transglutaminase 2 inhibitor — Phase 2 positive 2021, reduces villous atrophy); anti-IL-15 (AMG 714 for RCDII — Phase 2); oral gluten challenge + enzyme therapy combinations

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Acute flares self-resolve in 7-14 days without treatment; but urate crystal deposition continues; only 10-15% achieve serum urate <360 µmol/L spontaneously without ULT; progressive tophus formation and joint damage in untreated patients
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Allopurinol/febuxostat: serum urate target (<360 µmol/L) achieved in 60-70% if adequately dosed; treat-to-target with regular monitoring: 80-90% flare-free at 2 years; pegloticase: 40% complete tophus resolution at 6 months in refractory patients
diseases.gap_size: Very large — gout is one of the most undertreated diseases in medicine despite highly effective therapy
diseases.barrier_detail: Gout is widely (incorrectly) perceived as a diet-only disease by patients and many clinicians; ULT (urate-lowering therapy) is initiated in only 30-40% of eligible patients; of those who start ULT, only 30-40% reach serum urate target because doses are not escalated; gout management is the single most guideline-discordant area in rheumatology
diseases.last_soc_change: 2020 — ACR guidelines recommenced treat-to-target with regular dose titration of allopurinol; 2021 canakinumab guideline endorsement for frequent flare prevention; febuxostat CV safety concern (Black Box warning 2019 based on CARES trial); allopurinol standard since 1966
diseases.outdated_treatments: Colchicine monotherapy without ULT (treats symptoms, allows continued crystal deposition and joint damage); fixed-dose allopurinol without titration to serum urate target (most patients on 100-300mg when 600-900mg may be needed); NSAIDs long-term for flare prevention (instead of colchicine); diet modification as sole intervention in tophaceous gout
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: SGLT2 inhibitors (uricosuric effect reduces serum urate 0.5-1.2 mg/dL); cherry extract/anthocyanins (modest urate lowering in small RCTs; popular in patient communities); vitamin C supplementation (uricosuric effect, ~0.5 mg/dL reduction in trials); adequate hydration + dairy consumption
diseases.frontier_signal: URAT1 inhibitors (lesinurad withdrawn, but dotinurad advancing in Phase 3 in Japan); combination xanthine oxidase inhibitor + uricosuric approaches; IL-1 inhibitors (canakinumab, anakinra off-label) for polyarticular or NSAID/colchicine-contraindicated flares; uricase-based biologics (pegloticase — Phase 3 MIRROR RCT with immunosuppression co-administration)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: IBS is chronic and remitting; spontaneous 'remission' (symptom resolution without treatment) in ~30-40% at 2 years but symptom recurrence common; 10-20% develop persistent daily symptoms; <10% achieve permanent remission
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Low-FODMAP diet: 50-80% symptomatic response; gut-directed hypnotherapy: 70-80% response, durable at 5 years; CBT: 60-70% response; rifaximin for IBS-D: 40% vs 31% placebo (modest but significant); neuromodulators (amitriptyline, nortriptyline): 60-70% response in IBS-D/IBS-M
diseases.gap_size: Large — effective interventions exist but access to diet counselling, hypnotherapy, and CBT is limited; overreliance on antispasmodics and antidiarrheals
diseases.barrier_detail: Gut-directed hypnotherapy has the strongest long-term evidence of any IBS treatment but virtually no trained therapists; FODMAP diet requires skilled dietitian; pharmacotherapy (linaclotide, plecanatide, eluxadoline) effective but expensive; most patients manage with OTC antispasmodics indefinitely
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — AGA guidelines updated with neuromodulators as first-line; 2014 linaclotide/lubiprostone approved; 2015 eluxadoline for IBS-D; 2015 rifaximin for IBS-D; antispasmodics (hyoscine, mebeverine) essentially unchanged since 1960s-1970s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term loperamide as sole management in IBS-D without psychological or dietary intervention; alosetron (Lotronex — restricted prescribing programme due to ischaemic colitis; 5-HT3 antagonist with complex risk management); opioids for IBS pain (narcotic bowel syndrome risk — worsens visceral hypersensitivity with chronic use)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Low-FODMAP diet as primary (not just adjunct); fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) — RCTs conflicting but some positive for IBS-D; gut-directed hypnotherapy (Manchester protocol — most durable intervention in literature); bile acid sequestrants (colesevelam) for bile acid malabsorption subtype; peppermint oil (enteric-coated — meta-analysis superior to placebo, natural antispasmodic)
diseases.frontier_signal: FMT for IBS (PROFIT trial Phase 3); microbiome-targeted probiotics (VSL#3, Lactobacillus reuteri — inconsistent but growing evidence); tryptophan/serotonin pathway modulation; mast cell stabilisers (ebastine) for histamine-mediated IBS subtype; colonic sensory modulation via TRPV1 antagonists

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous drug-free remission: 2-10% in some cohorts over 5 years; disease-free survival in 15-20% at 20 years; renal lupus (LN) progression to ESRD in 10-20% within 20 years on standard care
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Triple therapy (hydroxychloroquine + MMF + belimumab): 43% complete renal response at 52 weeks (BLISS-LN); voclosporin + MMF: 40.8% complete renal response; anifrolumab (anti-IFNAR): 47.8% BICLA response in active SLE (TULIP-2); aggressive treat-to-target achieves low disease activity in 60-70%
diseases.gap_size: Large — hydroxychloroquine is dramatically underprescribed; newer biologics (belimumab, anifrolumab) reach very few eligible patients
diseases.barrier_detail: Hydroxychloroquine (the safest, most evidence-based lupus drug) was underprescribed even before COVID-19 controversy; biologics (belimumab, anifrolumab) approved since 2011 and 2021 respectively but used in <15% of eligible patients; racial disparities profound (Black/Hispanic patients have 2-3x higher disease severity and mortality but lower biologic access)
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — anifrolumab (Saphnelo, anti-IFN-type I) FDA approved — first new mechanism in decade; voclosporin (Lupkynis) for LN approved 2021; belimumab since 2011; hydroxychloroquine as backbone since 1950s; mycophenolate since 2000
diseases.outdated_treatments: Cyclophosphamide for mild-moderate LN (MMF proven equally effective with less toxicity in ALMS trial; cyclophosphamide retained for severe/rapidly progressive LN only); azathioprine induction therapy for active proliferative LN (inferior to MMF in RCTs); high-dose OCS as sole therapy for months without steroid-sparing agent
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Vitamin D optimisation (lupus patients characteristically deficient; observational data on disease activity reduction); omega-3 supplementation (anti-inflammatory, some trial data); N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — Phase 2 signal for fatigue and disease activity; exercise (aquatic and aerobic — reduces fatigue without triggering flares)
diseases.frontier_signal: CAR-T cell therapy (German case series: 5/5 patients with refractory SLE achieved drug-free remission — 2024 landmark data); CD19-directed CAR-T inducing B cell depletion and subsequent immune reset; obinutuzumab (anti-CD20) for LN Phase 3 (NOBILITY trial); dapirolizumab pegol (anti-CD40L); deucravacitinib (TYK2) for SLE Phase 3 positive

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Full recovery without intervention: <5% of adult ME/CFS patients; partial improvement in ~40% over 5 years; post-COVID ME/CFS appears to have similarly low spontaneous recovery rate
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: No disease-modifying treatment established; GET (graded exercise therapy) — former standard — shown harmful (PACE controversy, withdrawn from UK NICE guidance 2021); pacing (heart rate-based) prevents post-exertional malaise but not curative; low-dose naltrexone — ~30% improvement in small RCTs; rintatolimod (Ampligen) — Phase 3 failed FDA approval
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no effective treatment; historic misattribution to psychological causes delayed research by decades
diseases.barrier_detail: ME/CFS research has been systematically underfunded relative to disease burden (NIH funding $15M/year vs MS at $100M despite similar prevalence); PACE trial's GET/CBT approach adopted globally for years despite being harmful for many patients; NIH RECOVER initiative now represents largest-ever investment
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — NICE guidelines withdrew GET and CBT as primary treatments (major paradigm shift); 2024 NIH RECOVER Long COVID ME/CFS protocols underway; no approved pharmacotherapy exists; pacing as only widely recommended intervention
diseases.outdated_treatments: Graded Exercise Therapy (GET) — withdrawn from UK NICE guidance 2021 after evidence of harm in a significant proportion of patients; CBT framed as primary (not supportive) treatment implying psychological causation; antidepressants as standard first-line without evidence for underlying ME/CFS
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) — multiple small RCTs showing ~30% fatigue reduction; CoQ10 + NADH combination (Spanish trial: fatigue improvement); heart rate-guided pacing (Garmin + Polar strap protocols); mestinon (pyridostigmine — Phase 2 data for dysautonomia subtype); stellate ganglion block for COVID-ME/CFS dysautonomia
diseases.frontier_signal: BC007 (G-protein autoantibody neutralisation — Phase 2 for Long COVID/ME/CFS); BC-X (adrenergic autoantibody); AXA1125 (amino acid supplement targeting mitochondrial dysfunction — Phase 2 positive signal); rintatolimod; immunoadsorption (IMADS trial Germany — removing autoantibodies)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive structural damage (sacroiliac fusion, syndesmophytes) in majority without treatment; spontaneous radiographic remission essentially zero; ~10% achieve clinical remission without anti-inflammatory therapy
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: TNF inhibitors (infliximab, adalimumab): ASAS20 response in 60-70%, clinical remission (ASDAS-ID) in 20-40%; IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab): equivalent or superior efficacy; remission rates with early biologics in non-radiographic axSpA may reach 40-50%
diseases.gap_size: Large — most patients spend years on NSAIDs before biologic assessment; diagnostic delay averages 7-10 years
diseases.barrier_detail: Diagnostic delay of 7-10 years from symptom onset is the most significant modifiable factor; MRI-based diagnosis of nr-axSpA (non-radiographic) can enable earlier biologic initiation before structural damage; HLA-B27 testing remains underused in primary care assessment of inflammatory back pain
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — bimekizumab (dual IL-17A/F) approved for AS; 2019 secukinumab and ixekizumab established for AS/nr-axSpA; tofacitinib and upadacitinib (JAK inhibitors) added; TNF inhibitors as backbone since 2000
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term high-dose NSAID use without biologic assessment after 2+ NSAID failures; sulfasalazine/methotrexate for axial disease (ineffective for spinal involvement — standard for peripheral arthritis only, but sometimes prescribed for axial symptoms); glucocorticoid injections as sole intervention for sacroiliac joint involvement without systemic therapy
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (TNF inhibitor trial adjunct — exercise significantly increases response rates); hydrotherapy; standing desks and postural interventions; smoking cessation (accelerates radiographic progression); vitamin D optimisation
diseases.frontier_signal: Bimekizumab (dual IL-17A/F, superior radiographic inhibition vs IL-17A alone); JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib 2022 AS approval, filgotinib Phase 3); IL-23 inhibitors for peripheral/enthesitis manifestations; gut microbiome modulation (strong gut-axis link in axSpA)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Dry AMD: slow progressive loss; wet AMD progresses rapidly without treatment (50% of untreated reach legal blindness within 2 years); no spontaneous remission of either form
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab): 30-40% vision gain in wet AMD (MARINA, ANCHOR trials); maintain vision in 90%+ vs 62% in placebo; high-dose vitamin supplementation (AREDS2): 25% reduction in progression from intermediate to advanced dry AMD; port delivery system (Susvimo): 6-monthly refillable implant equivalent to monthly injections
diseases.gap_size: Large — treatment burden (monthly injections) is the primary barrier to adequate treatment in wet AMD; dry AMD has no proven disease-modifying treatment until 2023
diseases.barrier_detail: Monthly anti-VEGF injection burden leads to under-treatment in real-world settings; 50% of wet AMD patients in routine care lose vision compared to 5-10% in trials; geographic atrophy (dry AMD) had no approved treatment until pegcetacoplan (Syfovre, 2023)
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — pegcetacoplan (Syfovre, complement C3 inhibitor) and avacincaptad pegol (Izervay, complement C5 inhibitor) approved for geographic atrophy — first ever treatments for dry AMD; ranibizumab port delivery system (Susvimo) approved 2021; anti-VEGF since 2006
diseases.outdated_treatments: Photocoagulation laser for wet AMD (replaced by anti-VEGF since 2006; still used in some settings for extrafoveal disease); photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin (replaced by anti-VEGF except selected cases); vitamin E supplementation alone without the full AREDS2 formula
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: High-dose AREDS2 formula supplements (standard of care for intermediate AMD); lutein/zeaxanthin from diet or supplementation; Mediterranean diet pattern (observational data for AMD prevention); blue-light filtering lenses (theoretical, limited clinical evidence); omega-3 fatty acids (did not add benefit in AREDS2 but observational evidence supports)
diseases.frontier_signal: Subretinal gene therapy (RGX-314, ADVM-022 — delivering anti-VEGF gene to retinal cells, avoiding injections); AAV-CNGB3 for achromatopsia; faricimab (dual Ang2+VEGF blockade — extended dosing up to 16 weeks, TENAYA/LUCERNE); OPT-302 (VEGF-C/D trap) combination; complement pathway targeting for GA progression

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive optic nerve damage without treatment; average visual field loss 0.5-1.0 dB/year; blindness in 10-20% without treatment; no spontaneous recovery of lost vision
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: IOP-lowering drops: 20-30% IOP reduction; OHTS trial showed 50% risk reduction for glaucoma development in ocular hypertension; SLT (selective laser trabeculoplasty): IOP reduction 25-30%, equivalent to drops at 3 years (LiGHT trial); trabeculectomy: 35-45% IOP reduction, superior to drops in advanced disease
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — IOP control is achievable but compliance is the dominant barrier; neuroprotection remains an unmet need
diseases.barrier_detail: IOP-lowering prevents further damage but cannot restore lost vision; adherence to drop therapy is poor (50-70% of patients non-adherent at 1 year); SLT (laser) as first-line (recommended by LiGHT trial) not universally adopted; once-yearly drop (latanoprostene bunod) and sustained-release implants (Durysta) improving adherence
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — bimatoprost SR implant (Durysta) expanding to real-world use; 2021 Santen DE-117 sustained-release technology; 2019 rho kinase inhibitor (netarsudil/Rhopressa) approved — new mechanism; prostaglandin analogs as first-line since late 1990s; LiGHT trial 2019 established SLT as superior first-line to drops
diseases.outdated_treatments: Timolol as first-line monotherapy in patients with cardiovascular or respiratory contraindications (still common despite contraindications); brimonidine in children <5 years (CNS depression risk); aggressive IOP target without considering optic nerve status and progression rate
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) as first-line; aerobic exercise (reduces IOP 2-3 mmHg, possibly neuroprotective); caffeine avoidance in normal-tension glaucoma; ginkgo biloba (small RCTs showing visual field stabilisation in NTG); mitochondria-targeted neuroprotection
diseases.frontier_signal: ONS-5010 (intravitreal bevacizumab) for neovascular glaucoma; siRNA-based IOP reduction (monthly or quarterly dosing); stem cell-based trabecular meshwork regeneration; neuroprotective agents (CNTF, memantine — multiple Phase 3 trials failed; mechanism still being pursued); gene therapy for MYOC-associated glaucoma

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive with worsening glycaemic control; reversal essentially not possible with current treatments once established; spontaneous improvement only in cases of rapid glycaemic normalisation early in course
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Intensive glycaemic control in T1DM (DCCT): 60% reduction in neuropathy development; pain management (duloxetine, pregabalin): 30-50% pain reduction but no disease modification; alpha-lipoic acid IV: nerve conduction velocity improvement at 3 weeks (SYDNEY 2 trial); SGLT2 inhibitors: emerging data on neuropathy protection
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no disease-modifying therapy approved; pain treatment is the only intervention most patients receive
diseases.barrier_detail: Prevention through glycaemic control is highly effective but prevention means tight glycaemic control for decades; pain treatment gives symptomatic relief only; opioid prescribing for DPN pain remains common despite guidelines against it; few patients are offered alpha-lipoic acid which has the strongest disease-modification signal
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — FDA approved TENS device (Quell 2.0) for DPN pain; 2022 DPNP guidance from ADA updated; duloxetine and pregabalin as first-line since 2004-2005; no paradigm change for 20 years
diseases.outdated_treatments: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline) as first-line despite equivalent efficacy and better tolerability of SNRIs in elderly; opioids for DPN (long-term opioid therapy for DPN pain — no superiority to combination non-opioid therapy; guidelines explicitly discourage); vitamin B12 monitoring not performed despite metformin-induced B12 depletion in 5-40% of T2DM patients worsening neuropathy
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Alpha-lipoic acid 600mg/day (ALA — most studied supplement for DPN; IV form with best evidence but oral showing benefit in longer studies); benfotiamine (lipid-soluble B1 — BEDIP trial positive); transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS); near-infrared phototherapy; corneal confocal microscopy for early diagnosis enabling earlier intervention
diseases.frontier_signal: Aldose reductase inhibitors (epalrestat — approved in Asia, rejected FDA; Phase 3 ongoing globally); PARP inhibitors; BDNF analogs for nerve regeneration; sodium channel subtype-specific blockers (Nav1.7, Nav1.8) for pain without systemic effects; stem cell-based nerve regeneration for irreversible cases

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Without treatment, median survival from diagnosis 2.8 years (pre-modern-therapy era); with current therapy 5-year survival ~50-60%; spontaneous remission essentially unknown
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Triple upfront combination therapy (ambrisentan + tadalafil + selexipag): 41% reduction in clinical failure events (TRITON trial); macitentan + tadalafil initial combination: 41% risk reduction (AMBITION); lung transplantation: 50-60% 5-year survival
diseases.gap_size: Large — most patients still receive sequential monotherapy or dual therapy rather than upfront triple therapy
diseases.barrier_detail: Upfront combination therapy evidence is robust but drug costs (selexipag alone ~$150K/year) limit access; PAH is often missed or delayed in diagnosis (mean 2-year diagnostic delay); most patients are not treated at PAH expert centres where combination protocols are standard
diseases.last_soc_change: 2024 — sotatercept (Winrevair, activin signalling inhibitor) FDA approved — first new mechanism in 15 years; 2017 selexipag approved; upfront dual combination established 2015 (AMBITION); prostacyclin pathway drugs since 1995 (epoprostenol)
diseases.outdated_treatments: Sequential monotherapy add-on strategy (evidence strongly favours upfront combination, yet sequential approach remains common); calcium channel blockers without vasoreactivity testing (benefit only in the ~10% who are vasoreactive on right heart catheterisation; often prescribed empirically)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Supervised exercise rehabilitation (once contraindicated; now evidence-based, EFORT trial positive); sotatercept (now approved, anti-activin); iron supplementation (iron deficiency common in PAH, supplementation improves exercise tolerance); potassium channel openers
diseases.frontier_signal: Sotatercept (first disease-modifying candidate — reverses vascular remodelling, not just vasodilation); ralinepag (IP receptor agonist, sustained-release); rodatristat ethyl (tryptophan hydroxylase inhibitor for serotonin pathway); HIF inhibitors; cell therapy

diseases.disease: Lyme Disease / Post-Treatment Lyme Disease Syndrome (PTLDS)
diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Acute Lyme: spontaneous resolution in some untreated patients but dissemination and late manifestations develop; PTLDS (persistent symptoms after adequate antibiotics): affects 10-20% of patients; spontaneous resolution of PTLDS in 50-60% by 6 months
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Acute Lyme: 14-21 day doxycycline achieves 80-95% cure; PTLDS: no treatment shown superior to placebo in 4 large RCTs; prolonged antibiotic therapy (IDSA-condemned) not effective; symptom management only
diseases.gap_size: Large for PTLDS — the field lacks an evidence-based treatment and has been mired in controversy over mechanism and management
diseases.barrier_detail: PTLDS treatment is one of the most contentious areas in medicine; IDSA position (no benefit of prolonged antibiotics) vs ILADS position (chronic infection requiring extended treatment) — RCTs support IDSA; the patient experience of persistent symptoms is real but the mechanism may not be ongoing active infection; this creates a significant care gap for suffering patients
diseases.last_soc_change: 2020 — IDSA/AAN/ACR joint guidelines updated (no significant change in management; prolonged antibiotics explicitly not recommended); ongoing NIH investment in PTLDS mechanism research post-COVID-19 analogy with Long COVID
diseases.outdated_treatments: Prolonged oral/IV antibiotic therapy beyond standard course for PTLDS (4 large RCTs negative; significant antibiotic harm without benefit); hyperbaric oxygen therapy (no evidence); bismacine (injectable compound — FDA has issued warnings); samento/cat's claw without evidence
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Symptom-targeted management (sleep, pain, fatigue — same interventions as ME/CFS); cognitive rehabilitation for brain fog; immunomodulatory approaches (under investigation); doxycycline repurposing for anti-inflammatory (non-antibiotic) effects in PTLDS
diseases.frontier_signal: mRNA Lyme vaccine (Pfizer/Valneva VLA15/PF-06882961 — Phase 3 VALOR trial 2023, 89% efficacy vs Lyme disease — major prevention breakthrough); host-directed anti-inflammatory therapy for PTLDS; PCR-based diagnostics improving sensitivity for delayed diagnosis

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Chronic HBV: spontaneous HBsAg loss (functional cure) in 0.5-2%/year on antiviral therapy, 0.1-0.5%/year without treatment; HBeAg seroconversion spontaneous in 5-10%/year; progression to cirrhosis in 20-25% of untreated chronic HBV over 20 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Current antivirals (entecavir, tenofovir): suppress HBV DNA to undetectable in >95% but do not clear cccDNA reservoir; functional cure (HBsAg loss) in 1-3%/year on antiviral; combination anti-HBsAg approaches in trials showing 20-40% functional cure rates at 48 weeks
diseases.gap_size: Very large — suppressive therapy prevents cirrhosis but does not cure; functional cure rate on current therapy is very low; treatment gap in LMIC enormous (only 30% of eligible patients treated globally)
diseases.barrier_detail: The cccDNA reservoir in hepatocytes cannot be cleared by current antivirals; cure requires either HBsAg clearance or cccDNA elimination; 296 million people with chronic HBV globally, only ~30% on treatment; HBV vaccination has transformed prevention but treatment of existing infection remains suboptimal
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — AASLD and EASL guidelines both shifted toward expanding treatment eligibility regardless of ALT or fibrosis stage; tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) replaced TDF as preferred tenofovir form 2016; entecavir and TDF as first-line since 2005-2008; no paradigm change for >15 years
diseases.outdated_treatments: Lamivudine monotherapy (high resistance rate — 70% at 5 years; replaced by entecavir/tenofovir); conventional interferon-alfa (replaced by PEG-IFN; more side effects, lower efficacy); sequential monotherapy (resistance development) vs immediate high-barrier-to-resistance agents
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Lifestyle modification (alcohol cessation critical; reduces cirrhosis and HCC risk even on antiviral); surveillance protocols for HCC (underperformed globally); coffee consumption (3+ cups/day: 40% HCC risk reduction in observational data)
diseases.frontier_signal: RNA interference therapy (vir-2218, RG6346 — HBsAg knockdown enabling immune reconstitution; Phase 2 positive); capsid assembly modulators (NVR 3-778, ABI-H0731); HBsAg-targeting antibodies; therapeutic vaccines (HepTcell, GS-4774); CRISPR targeting cccDNA in preclinical models

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous resolution without intervention rare; weight loss of 10-15% reduces AHI by 25-50% in obese patients; mild OSA may resolve with weight loss; moderate-severe OSA essentially irreversible without intervention
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: CPAP: eliminates apnoeic events (AHI <5) in 95%+ when used; improves BP 2-3 mmHg, Epworth sleepiness, QoL; mandibular advancement device (MAD): reduces AHI 50-60% in mild-moderate, less effective in severe; bariatric surgery: AHI reduction 80-85% (OSA resolution in 40-50%); hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire): AHI reduction 68% at 12 months
diseases.gap_size: Very large — CPAP adherence is the dominant problem; 30-50% of CPAP-prescribed patients are non-adherent; alternative approaches (MAD, positional therapy, weight loss) underused in non-adherent patients
diseases.barrier_detail: CPAP adherence is the single largest challenge in sleep medicine; 50% of patients discontinue CPAP within 1-3 years; inadequate follow-up support and lack of alternatives to CPAP for non-adherent patients; hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire) is highly effective in CPAP-intolerant patients but vastly underutilised
diseases.last_soc_change: 2024 — tirzepatide showing 60-70% AHI reduction in obese OSA patients (SURMOUNT-OSA trial); 2023 Inspire (HNS) expanded indications; 2014 Inspire FDA approved; CPAP as standard since 1981; essentially unchanged paradigm for 40 years in most clinical settings
diseases.outdated_treatments: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) surgery without HNS assessment (response rates 40-50%, durable in only 30%; should be last resort after HNS evaluation); supplemental oxygen instead of CPAP (treats hypoxia but not arousals/sleep fragmentation); antidepressants for OSA (not indicated as primary treatment)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: GLP-1/GIP agonists (SURMOUNT-OSA: tirzepatide reduced AHI by 60-68% in obese OSA — 2024 landmark result); myofunctional therapy (oropharyngeal exercises — 39% AHI reduction in meta-analysis); positional therapy devices for positional OSA; mouth taping (small series for mouth-breathers)
diseases.frontier_signal: Tirzepatide for OSA (FDA approval for OSA without surgery expected 2024-2025 based on SURMOUNT-OSA); AD109 (arousal threshold-raising pharmacotherapy combining atomoxetine + oxybutynin — Phase 3 MARIPOSA trial); reboxetine + antimuscarinic combinations; transoral robotic tongue base reduction

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Endometriosis lesions may partially regress after menopause; spontaneous resolution of symptoms rare; recurrence rate after surgery 20-40% at 2 years and 50-60% at 5 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Excision surgery achieves pain relief in 60-80% at 6 months but 50% recurrence at 5 years; GnRH agonists + add-back therapy: 70-90% pain reduction but oestrogen-deprivation side effects; combined OCP: 50-60% pain reduction; elagolix (Orilissa): 43% reduction in dysmenorrhoea vs placebo (ELARIS EM-I)
diseases.gap_size: Very large — diagnostic delay averages 7-10 years from symptom onset; no disease-modifying treatment; high recurrence after surgery
diseases.barrier_detail: Diagnostic delay of 7-10 years is the largest modifiable factor; endometriosis dismissed as 'normal period pain'; no non-surgical diagnostic test; all medical treatments are suppressive not curative; recurrence after surgery is universal without continued medical suppression
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — linzagolix (Yselty, GnRH antagonist) approved in Europe; 2021 relugolix combination (Myfembree) FDA approved; elagolix (Orilissa) 2018; GnRH agonists standard since 1980s; excision surgery approach unchanged for decades
diseases.outdated_treatments: Danazol (androgenic; significant side effects including virilisation, liver toxicity; replaced by GnRH analogues and GnRH antagonists); medroxyprogesterone acetate injections as long-term therapy without bone density monitoring; diagnostic laparoscopy without concurrent excision (two-step procedure causing additional delay to treatment)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Dietary anti-inflammatory approaches (omega-3, low-inflammatory diet — observational data on symptom reduction); N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — Italian RCT showing endometrioma regression; melatonin (anti-inflammatory, analgesic — small RCTs positive); cannabidiol (CBD) for pelvic pain — Phase 2 underway
diseases.frontier_signal: Dichloroacetate (DCA) — metabolic reprogramming of endometriosis cells (Phase 2); progesterone receptor modulators (mifepristone low-dose; ulipristal — EU regulatory issues after liver toxicity); stem cell-based endometrial repair; JAK inhibitors for ectopic lesion suppression

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive natural history; symptoms worsen in 50-60% over 5 years without treatment; spontaneous improvement in 20-30% (particularly mild symptoms); acute urinary retention risk ~1-2%/year
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Combination alpha-blocker + 5-ARI (tamsulosin + dutasteride): reduces prostate volume 25-30%, acute urinary retention risk 67% (CombAT trial); minimally invasive procedures (Rezum, Urolift, Aquablation): IPSS improvement 50-60%, preserving ejaculatory function better than TURP; TURP: gold standard, 70-80% symptom improvement
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — treatment is effective but procedure uptake and pharmacotherapy titration is suboptimal; many patients overtreated with TURP when less invasive options appropriate
diseases.barrier_detail: Alpha-blockers provide symptomatic relief but do not address prostate volume in large glands (5-ARI needed); TURP remains widely used when Rezum/Urolift preserve sexual function with similar efficacy; pharmacotherapy stepped-up without referral in cases where early MIS procedure would preserve more function
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — Aquablation FDA approved; 2019 Rezum (water vapour therapy) established; 2016 Urolift established; 5-ARI added to alpha-blocker standard since CombAT trial 2008; alpha-blockers first-line since 1990s; TURP gold standard since 1960s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Open prostatectomy for glands <100g (TURP or HoLEP preferable); terazosin/doxazosin as first-line alpha-blockers (uroselective agents tamsulosin/silodosin have better BP side effect profile); saw palmetto (multiple large RCTs null including STEP and CAMUS trials — yet widely used)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Saw palmetto (STEP and CAMUS trials both null but widely used); pumpkin seed oil (very limited trial data); dietary: lycopene, zinc; pelvic floor exercises for mild overactive bladder component; beta-3 agonist (mirabegron) for mixed OAB/BPH symptoms
diseases.frontier_signal: Convective water vapour ablation (Rezum) expanding; high-frequency focused ultrasound (HIFU) for BPH; intraprostate botulinum toxin injection (Phase 2 data); prostate arterial embolisation (PAE) for large glands; 5G/robotic-assisted Aquablation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous resolution by adulthood in majority (persistent adult acne in 12-22% of women, 3% of men); moderate-severe acne rarely self-resolves; scarring occurs in up to 95% without adequate treatment
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Oral isotretinoin: 85-90% achieve sustained remission (acne-free at 5 years) after one course; relapse requiring retreat in 15-20%; topical adapalene + benzoyl peroxide: 65-70% reduction in inflammatory lesions at 12 weeks
diseases.gap_size: Large — isotretinoin is highly effective but dramatically underused due to iPLEDGE programme barriers and pregnancy concerns; patients spend years on antibiotics instead
diseases.barrier_detail: The iPLEDGE programme creates substantial barriers to isotretinoin access (monthly blood tests, pregnancy tests, pharmacy restrictions); this leads to years of antibiotic courses creating antibiotic resistance and allowing scarring to progress; acne is not considered a 'serious' disease despite causing significant psychological morbidity and permanent scarring
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — FDA approved clascoterone (Winlevi) — first androgen receptor inhibitor for acne; 2021 Accutane biosimilar access improved; 2019 AAD guidelines updated; isotretinoin approved 1982; tetracycline antibiotics for acne since 1950s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Prolonged oral antibiotic monotherapy without topical retinoid co-prescription (antibiotic resistance — P. acnes resistance to tetracyclines increasing; AAD guidelines recommend antibiotic + BPO or retinoid combination, never antibiotic alone); topical antibiotic monotherapy without BPO (same resistance issue); iodine-based products; comedone extractions as sole intervention
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Low-glycaemic diet (RCT data: significant reduction in inflammatory lesion count); dairy reduction (observational data consistent with RCTs); zinc supplementation (equivalent to some antibiotics in trials, particularly for inflammatory acne); spearmint tea (anti-androgenic effect in small RCTs for hormonal acne); niacinamide topical
diseases.frontier_signal: Clascoterone (Winlevi — topical anti-androgen without systemic hormonal effects); trifarotene (fourth-generation retinoid for truncal acne); phage therapy targeting C. acnes; microbiome modulation; intense pulsed light (IPL) and photodynamic therapy for recurrence prevention post-isotretinoin

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive lung function decline; no spontaneous remission; historically median survival 30 years; now >50 years with CFTR modulators for eligible genotypes
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI, Trikafta): FEV1 improvement 14-15 percentage points, sweat chloride normalisation in F508del homozygous patients (AURORA trial); 80-90% of CF patients in US have eligible genotypes; 'functional cure' in many patients — near-normal lung function in those starting young
diseases.gap_size: Near-zero biological gap for CFTR-modulator-eligible patients (10-15% have non-eligible mutations); very large access gap globally
diseases.barrier_detail: Trikafta is among the most expensive drugs ever approved (~$330K/year); most middle-income countries have no access; 10-15% of CF patients (class I nonsense mutations) are not eligible for CFTR modulators and remain without disease-modifying therapy; expanded mRNA/gene therapy approach needed for ineligible patients
diseases.last_soc_change: 2019 — Trikafta (elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor) FDA approved — arguably the most transformative disease-modifying approval in CF history; 2015 lumacaftor-ivacaftor; 2012 ivacaftor (G551D only); lung transplantation as sole 'curative' option for decades; DNase, tobramycin inhaled, hypertonic saline as symptomatic standard since 1990s-2000s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Lung transplantation as only endpoint in all CF patients (now reserved for those without modulator access or ineligible genotypes); systemic corticosteroids for pulmonary exacerbations in CF (evidence against; inhaled preferred); prophylactic antibiotics without culture-guided selection (increasing MDR organism risk)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise rehabilitation (particularly aerobic + strength — evidence-based for lung function preservation); airway clearance techniques (chest physiotherapy, oscillatory PEP, high-frequency chest wall oscillation); nutritional optimisation; CFTR modulator combination with NAC for residual mucus dysfunction
diseases.frontier_signal: mRNA-based CFTR replacement (Translate Bio/Sanofi — clinical trials paused 2022 but technology advancing); gene editing (base editing, prime editing for nonsense mutations — preclinical); next-generation CFTR modulators (vanzacaftor-tezacaftor-deutivacaftor — Phase 3 RIDGELINE trials); lentiviral gene therapy (UK Consortium Phase 2)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous resolution at 12 months: ~50-70% of patients; persistent symptoms at 2 years in 30-50%; severely debilitating long COVID in 10-15% at 1 year without intervention
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: No approved pharmacotherapy; Paxlovid (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir) — PANORAMIC trial showed no reduction in Long COVID risk at 6 months if started in acute phase; BC007 (autoantibody neutraliser) — Phase 2 signal; low-dose naltrexone — observational data positive; antihistamine combination (famotidine + cetirizine) — anecdotal
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no proven pharmacotherapy; enormous research investment but no approved treatment as of mid-2026
diseases.barrier_type: biological (mechanism not fully understood) + practice (no evidence-based protocol)
diseases.barrier_detail: Long COVID is a heterogeneous syndrome with multiple proposed mechanisms (viral persistence, autoimmunity, microclotting, dysautonomia, mitochondrial dysfunction); no single treatment works for all patients; fragmented care with poor coordination; many clinicians still do not acknowledge Long COVID as a diagnosis
diseases.last_soc_change: 2024 — NIH RECOVER initiative released clinical trial protocols for LDN, anticoagulation, anti-viral, and SSRIs; no approved treatment exists; WHO case definition 2021; CDC guidance 2022
diseases.outdated_treatments: Advising rest alone (worsens deconditioning without addressing underlying mechanisms); graded exercise therapy (as in ME/CFS — potentially harmful in post-exertional malaise phenotype); dismissing symptoms as psychosomatic without investigation of autonomic function, inflammatory markers, microclot testing
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Pacing (heart rate-based, matching POTS/dysautonomia protocols); H1+H2 antihistamine combination (famotidine + loratadine/cetirizine — anecdotal but mechanistically plausible for mast cell activation); low-dose naltrexone (LDN); BC007 (G-protein-coupled receptor autoantibody neutralisation); ivermectin (ongoing controversy — TOGETHER trial no benefit for acute, sparse Long COVID data); stellate ganglion block for POTS/dysautonomia subtype
diseases.frontier_signal: BC007 (G-PCRAB neutralisation — Phase 2); AXA1125 (amino acid EAA for mitochondrial support); RSLV-132 (RNase-Fc fusion for RNA-containing immune complexes); Paxlovid for Long COVID viral persistence hypothesis (PTRB trial); SSRI/SNRI for serotonin depletion hypothesis (RECOVER-VITAL trial); anticoagulation/fibrinolysis for microclot hypothesis

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Low-grade (Gleason 6/Grade Group 1): active surveillance appropriate in 40-50% — many will never need treatment; high-grade: lethal without treatment within 5-10 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Localised: surgery (radical prostatectomy) or radiotherapy — 90-95% disease-specific survival at 10 years; mCSPC: darolutamide + docetaxel + ADT (ARASENS): 32% OS improvement; enzalutamide/abiraterone + ADT as standard 2019-2021; PARP inhibitors for BRCA-mutant: olaparib — 34% OS benefit (PROfound)
diseases.gap_size: Large — active surveillance underused in low-risk disease (overtreatment); ADT monotherapy still used where combination therapy is standard
diseases.barrier_detail: Active surveillance in low-risk PCa is guideline-endorsed but only 50-60% of eligible patients are managed this way (radical treatment used unnecessarily with significant sexual/urinary side effects); intensified ADT combinations are standard of care for mHSPC but ADT monotherapy still prescribed
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022-2023 — darolutamide + ADT + docetaxel triplet; PARP inhibitors for HRR-mutant mCRPC; 2021 lutetium-177-PSMA-617 FDA approved; 2019 first-line enzalutamide/abiraterone for mHSPC; PSA testing debate ongoing
diseases.outdated_treatments: ADT monotherapy for newly diagnosed metastatic PCa (combination with docetaxel or androgen receptor inhibitor is now standard); radical prostatectomy for all low-risk disease without active surveillance option; routine PSA screening without risk stratification (USPSTF Grade C recommendation reflecting overdiagnosis concerns)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (reduces treatment side effects, improves QoL, some data on cancer-specific survival); tomato/lycopene consumption (observational data on risk reduction); vitamin D + calcium for bone protection during ADT; PSMA-targeted radiopharmaceuticals (lutetium-177-PSMA expanding to earlier settings)
diseases.frontier_signal: PSMA-targeted therapy expanding (AC225-PSMA for alpha-particle therapy; PSMA-PET guided micrometastasis treatment); CAR-T targeting PSMA; bispecific antibodies (pasotuxizumab); PARP inhibitor + anti-PD-1 combinations for BRCA-mutant disease

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Indolent NHL (follicular): spontaneous remission in 20-30% of early-stage, allowing watch-and-wait; aggressive NHL (DLBCL): rapidly lethal without treatment in weeks-months
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: DLBCL with R-CHOP: 60-70% cure rate; Pola-R-CHP (polatuzumab): 27% improvement in 2-year PFS (POLARIX); DLBCL relapsed: axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) CAR-T: 40-50% long-term remission; follicular lymphoma: obinutuzumab + chemotherapy 85-90% response, durable
diseases.gap_size: Variable by subtype; DLBCL has genuinely curative potential; indolent NHL remains manageable but generally incurable with current therapy
diseases.barrier_detail: CAR-T therapy is transformative in relapsed DLBCL but manufacturing time (2-3 weeks), cost ($400K+), and centre requirements limit access; bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab, glofitamab) offer off-the-shelf alternative
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022-2023 — bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab, glofitamab) approved for relapsed DLBCL; CAR-T as second-line (ZUMA-7, TRANSFORM) since 2022; Pola-R-CHP first-line 2023; R-CHOP since 2000 (CHOP since 1970s)
diseases.outdated_treatments: CHOP without rituximab in CD20+ NHL (R-CHOP demonstrated superior survival in 2002, yet rituximab-free regimens occasionally still used); stem cell transplant as second-line without CAR-T consideration in eligible patients
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise during chemotherapy (reduces fatigue, maintains QoL); immunonutrition; lymphoma-specific psycho-oncology support; infection prophylaxis optimisation during rituximab-based therapy
diseases.frontier_signal: Bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab subcutaneous 2023; glofitamab fixed-duration 2023); CAR-T optimisation (allogeneic off-the-shelf); anti-CD47 (magrolimab + rituximab Phase 3); PD-1/PD-L1 combinations; BTK degraders (non-covalent for ibrutinib-resistant CLL/NHL)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Early-stage (Rai 0-I): watch-and-wait appropriate in 50-70%; spontaneous remission extremely rare; median time to treatment in Rai Stage 0: 10+ years; Richter's transformation (5-10%) extremely poor prognosis
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Venetoclax + obinutuzumab (CLL14): 2-year fixed duration, MRD-undetectable remission in 36%, PFS benefit sustained at 5 years vs chlorambucil; ibrutinib continuous: PFS benefit but not curative; BRUIN CLL trial (pirtobrutinib): 73% ORR in ibrutinib-resistant CLL
diseases.gap_size: Large — chlorambucil still used in some elderly patients; BTK inhibitor choice and venetoclax combination timing are practice-variable
diseases.barrier_detail: Chemoimmunotherapy (FCR, BR) largely replaced by BTK inhibitors and venetoclax but chlorambucil still used where cost is a barrier; the 'best drug at first relapse' question is evolving rapidly; CAR-T not yet standard for CLL
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — pirtobrutinib (non-covalent BTKi) for ibrutinib-resistant CLL; 2020 venetoclax + obinutuzumab fixed-duration; 2019 ibrutinib first-line approved; fludarabine + cyclophosphamide + rituximab (FCR) as standard 2006-2019; chlorambucil since 1950s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Chlorambucil as sole agent in eligible younger patients (FCR or BTKi clearly superior); CHOP chemotherapy for CLL (not appropriate); rituximab monotherapy (combination with chemotherapy or BTKi superior)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Infection prophylaxis and vaccination (particularly COVID-19 booster strategies for immunocompromised CLL patients); exercise during watch-and-wait; IVIG for hypogammaglobulinaemia prevention of infections
diseases.frontier_signal: Pirtobrutinib (BRUIN — non-covalent BTKi overcoming C481S resistance); zanubrutinib + venetoclax combinations; CAR-T for CLL (TRANSCEND CLL 004 — lisocabtagene maraleucel); bispecific antibodies; MRD-guided treatment duration protocols

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Essentially zero; MGUS (precursor) progresses to myeloma in 1%/year; median survival without treatment <1 year once symptomatic
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Daratumumab + VRd (daratumumab + bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone) induction + autologous SCT: 66% MRD-negative at 24 months (PERSEUS); CAR-T (idecabtagene vicleucel, ciltacabtagene autoleucel): 71-75% overall response, 28-33% CR in heavily pretreated patients; 5-year survival now ~55% (vs ~20% in 2000)
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — outcomes dramatically improved but cure remains elusive; CAR-T access critical unmet need
diseases.barrier_detail: BCMA-targeted CAR-T therapy is transformative but manufacturing delays (6+ weeks), $420K+ cost, and limited centre access restrict use; bispecific antibodies (teclistamab) offer alternative; a functional cure remains an aspiration not a reality for most patients
diseases.last_soc_change: 2024 — talquetamab (GPRC5D bispecific) approved; 2022 ciltacabtagene autoleucel CAR-T approved; 2021 idecabtagene vicleucel CAR-T first-ever for MM; 2019 daratumumab as frontline; bortezomib/lenalidomide backbone since 2003-2006
diseases.outdated_treatments: Melphalan + prednisone as first-line in transplant-eligible patients (triplet/quadruplet regimens clearly superior); thalidomide in the era of lenalidomide (more side effects, equivalent efficacy); bisphosphonate without RANKL inhibitor discussion in bone disease
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise during myeloma therapy (MASCOT trial — reduces fatigue, increases OS signal); neridronate/zoledronic acid for bone protection; gut microbiome optimisation for IMiD response prediction; vitamin D for IMID-related bone metabolism
diseases.frontier_signal: BCMA/CD3 bispecific antibodies (teclistamab, elranatamab — off-the-shelf monthly subcutaneous); GPRC5D targeting; tandem CAR constructs; CAR-T as frontline (MonumenTAL, KarMMa-3 Phase 3 early line); anti-CD38 + BCMA combinations; minimal residual disease (MRD)-guided consolidation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: No spontaneous remission; progressive organ damage; median survival historically 40-60 years but improving; vaso-occlusive crises (VOC) in 1-4 per year average
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Haematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT): curative in 90-95% if matched sibling donor available; hydroxyurea reduces VOC frequency 50%, ACS risk 50%; gene therapy (exagamglogene autotemcel [exa-cel]/Casgevy): 30/31 patients free from severe VOC through 24 months; betibeglogene (Zynteglo): 90% reduction in severe VOC
diseases.gap_size: Very large — HSCT is curative but only 20% have matched donors; gene therapy is curative but costs $2-3M; hydroxyurea is dramatically underprescribed
diseases.barrier_detail: Hydroxyurea is inexpensive, highly effective, and generic but prescribed to only 25-35% of eligible SCD patients (racial and healthcare access disparities prominent); gene therapies (Casgevy, Zynteglo) approved December 2023 represent first curative options without matched donor — but $2-3M price tag makes access essentially impossible without negotiation
diseases.last_soc_change: December 2023 — FDA approved exagamglogene autotemcel (Casgevy, CRISPR-based) and betibeglogene (Zynteglo) — first CRISPR-based gene therapy approvals anywhere in medicine; voxelotor (2019); crizanlizumab (2019); hydroxyurea standard since 1998
diseases.outdated_treatments: Blood transfusion as sole acute management without concurrent chelation for iron overload in chronic transfusion patients; hydroxyurea avoidance due to misperceived teratogenicity concerns in non-pregnant patients; opioid-centric pain management without multimodal approach (NSAIDs, regional analgesia, SNRIs)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: L-glutamine supplementation (FDA approved 2017 for SCD — reduces VOC 25%); magnesium pidolate (membrane stabilisation — Phase 2); inhaled nitric oxide for acute ACS; omega-3 (reduces pro-inflammatory red cell sickling); hydroxyurea dose optimisation by HbF response
diseases.frontier_signal: Casgevy (CRISPR-Cas9 reactivating HbF — first human CRISPR therapy approved); Zynteglo (lentiviral HBB gene addition); mitapivat (pyruvate kinase activator — Phase 3 RISE UP); inclacumab (P-selectin inhibitor, Phase 3 FREEDOM); etavopivat (Phase 2)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous remission uncommon (<5%); progressive joint damage in 40-60% without adequate treatment; enthesitis, dactylitis, axial involvement accumulate over time
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: IL-17 inhibitors: ACR50 response 58-62%, MDA (minimal disease activity) in 40-50%; IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab): similar efficacy with better skin clearance; JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib): ACR50 71% at 26 weeks; drug-free remission in ~15% in early PsA with T2T
diseases.gap_size: Large — most patients are undertreated on methotrexate or NSAIDs alone; treat-to-target poorly implemented
diseases.barrier_detail: Diagnostic delay (mean 5-7 years from symptom onset to PsA diagnosis); methotrexate has modest efficacy in PsA but is gatekept as mandatory step before biologic access in many health systems; treat-to-target (MDA as goal) poorly implemented; most patients cycle through multiple NSAIDs before biologic access
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — upadacitinib first-line biologic approved for PsA; deucravacitinib (TYK2) Phase 3 data; 2020 guselkumab/risankizumab for PsA; tofacitinib/ixekizumab since 2017-2018; anti-TNF standard since 2003
diseases.outdated_treatments: Oral corticosteroids for PsA flare management without biologic escalation (systemic corticosteroids worsen psoriasis skin component on tapering); sulfasalazine as sole DMARD in polyarticular PsA without biologic consideration; leflunomide (modest efficacy; maintained despite better alternatives)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise and physiotherapy (reduce pain, maintain function in PsA); dietary interventions (anti-inflammatory, weight management — obesity impairs biologic response); nail care for nail psoriasis component; smoking cessation (smokers have significantly worse PsA outcomes)
diseases.frontier_signal: Deucravacitinib (TYK2 inhibitor) for skin + joint; bimekizumab for PsA Phase 3 data; sonelokimab (dual IL-17A/F nanobody); integrin inhibitors; anti-TNF + anti-IL-17 combination; CRISPR-based immune modulation in research

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous remission essentially zero in class III/IV LN; progression to ESRD in 10-20% at 10 years on standard therapy; renal flares in 30-50% of patients achieving initial response
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Voclosporin + MMF + low-dose OCS: 40.8% complete renal response at 52 weeks (AURORA 1); belimumab + SOC: 43% primary efficacy renal response (BLISS-LN); obinutuzumab + MMF + SOC: superior histological response vs rituximab (NOBILITY)
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — better outcomes available with triple therapy but access and racial disparities limit implementation
diseases.barrier_detail: Black and Hispanic patients have significantly worse LN outcomes and are underrepresented in landmark trials; voclosporin and belimumab add significant cost; cyclophosphamide still used in some settings where MMF is equally effective in non-Black patients (Euro-Lupus low-dose equivalent); racial disparities in specialist access
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — voclosporin (Lupkynis) approved; belimumab IV added for LN (BLISS-LN); MMF established as standard for induction since ACR 2012 guidelines; cyclophosphamide from 1986 (NIH protocol)
diseases.outdated_treatments: High-dose cyclophosphamide (original NIH protocol) for all LN (Euro-Lupus low-dose equivalent efficacy with less toxicity); azathioprine for induction in active LN (maintenance only; inferior to MMF for induction); OCS monotherapy for mild class II LN without hydroxychloroquine
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Hydroxychloroquine optimised dosing (renal-protective independent of disease activity — weight-based dosing 5mg/kg preferred); vitamin D optimisation; dietary: low-sodium for hypertension management in LN; ACEI/ARB for proteinuria reduction
diseases.frontier_signal: CAR-T for SLE/LN (German case series: drug-free remission achieved); obinutuzumab (anti-CD20 glycoengineered — Phase 3 NOBILITY data positive); dapirolizumab pegol (anti-CD40L); anifrolumab extension to LN (Phase 2 TULIP-LN)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Chronic and progressive; glandular dysfunction (dry eyes/mouth) irreversible in most; fatigue and systemic features fluctuate; lymphoma risk 5-15x general population; spontaneous full remission rare
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Hydroxychloroquine: limited systemic benefit; rituximab: parotid gland improvement and fatigue in some trials but primary endpoint failure in Phase 3 (TEARS, TRACTISS); no disease-modifying therapy approved; best outcomes with early systematic sicca management
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no approved disease-modifying therapy; symptomatic management only for glandular features
diseases.barrier_detail: Sjögren's research has been hampered by heterogeneous disease and lack of validated endpoints; rituximab failed multiple Phase 3 trials despite positive Phase 2 data (endpoint selection problem); anti-Ro/SSA positivity enriches trials but treatment effects still modest; diagnosis itself is delayed 4-7 years on average
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — iscalimab (anti-CD40) Phase 3 data; no disease-modifying therapy approved; EULAR recommendations updated 2020; hydroxychloroquine as standard systemic agent since 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Systemic corticosteroids for sicca features (no evidence of benefit for glandular dysfunction; risk-benefit unfavourable); pilocarpine at fixed doses without titration to tolerance (dose titration allows benefit in more patients); ophthalmology referral without comprehensive sicca management protocol
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Autologous serum eye drops (highly effective for severe keratoconjunctivitis sicca — only available in centres with preparation capability); oral cevimeline or pilocarpine (secretagogues — underused); topical 0.1% ciclosporin ophthalmic emulsion (Restasis); salivary gland stimulation with electric toothbrush
diseases.frontier_signal: Ianalumab (anti-BAFF-R — targeting B cell survival; Phase 3 TWINSS trial); iscalimab (CD40 blockade — Phase 3 IMI-FORM); telitacicept (TACI-Ig fusion — dual BAFF/APRIL blockade; Phase 2 positive); LY3471851 (KLK5 inhibitor for dry eye); stem cell therapy for parotid gland regeneration

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: No spontaneous remission; lifelong clotting factor deficiency; joint damage progressive with each bleed without treatment; with prophylaxis, normal lifespan achievable
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Emicizumab prophylaxis (HAVEN studies): 87% reduction in bleeds vs on-demand FVIII; fitusiran (antithrombin RNAi): monthly subcutaneous injection, 89% reduction in bleeds; gene therapy (valoctocogene roxaparvovec): 94% reduction in bleeds at 5 years (GENEr8-1)
diseases.gap_size: Large — gene therapy offers functional cure but access is essentially the entire gap
diseases.barrier_detail: Gene therapy for haemophilia A (valoctocogene roxaparvovec/Roctavian, FDA approved 2023) and B (fidanacogene elaparvovec/Beqvez) represent functional cures in eligible patients but cost $2.9-3.5M; IgG immune responses limit retreatment; durability questions persist at 5 years; most patients in LMICs remain on factor concentrates
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — fidanacogene elaparvovec (Beqvez, gene therapy for HemB) FDA approved; 2023 valoctocogene roxaparvovec (Roctavian) FDA approved for HemA; fitusiran (antithrombin siRNA) 2023; emicizumab (Hemlibra) 2017; factor concentrates standard since 1960s
diseases.outdated_treatments: On-demand factor replacement therapy only without prophylaxis (bleeds allowed until treatment — joint damage accumulates; prophylaxis is now standard for severe haemophilia); plasma-derived factor concentrates where recombinant available (HIV/HCV transmission historical concern; recombinant preferred)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Physiotherapy for synovitis and joint rehabilitation; pain management with COX-2 inhibitors (NSAIDs contraindicated); dental care intensification (prevention reduces oral bleeding complications); exercise (age-appropriate haemophilia exercise programmes)
diseases.frontier_signal: Gene therapy for both HemA and HemB (long-term durability data pending; combination with immune tolerance strategies for anti-AAV-reactive patients); CRISPR-based B cell-targeted FVIII/FIX gene insertion; mRNA therapy for haemophilia (Moderna mRNA-3927 for propionic acidemia as proof of concept pathway); bispecific antibodies targeting tenase complex

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Nail psoriasis: spontaneous improvement in ~20-30% but rarely complete; palmoplantar pustulosis: highly refractory, spontaneous remission in <10%; both cause significant functional impairment
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: IL-17 inhibitors: 50-60% NAPSI75 (nail psoriasis) response at 24 weeks; secukinumab PPPP trial: 67% PPPASI75 for pustulosis; spesolimab for GPP: 54% GPPGA 0 at 1 week (EFFISAYIL-1)
diseases.gap_size: Large — nail and palmoplantar subtypes are therapeutic challenges, often undertreated
diseases.barrier_detail: Nail and palmoplantar psoriasis are resistant to topicals but patients are often managed with topicals alone for years; these subtypes cause disproportionate functional disability; specialist referral and biologic access are the primary gaps
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — spesolimab (Spevigo) FDA approved for generalised pustular psoriasis flares — first targeted therapy specifically for GPP; IL-17/IL-23 inhibitors established for nail/PP variants 2015-2020
diseases.outdated_treatments: Coal tar-based preparations for palmoplantar psoriasis (low efficacy, cosmetically unacceptable, carcinogenic with UV combination); acitretin monotherapy without biologic consideration in refractory cases; repeated topical corticosteroid courses without disease-modifying reassessment
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Targeted phototherapy (excimer laser for localised plaque); anti-inflammatory diet; emollient optimisation; occupational therapy for palmoplantar disability
diseases.frontier_signal: Spesolimab prevention of GPP flares (EFFISAYIL-2 Phase 3 positive 2023); IL-36 pathway targeting for pustular variants; orismilast (PDE4 inhibitor, topical) for palmoplantar psoriasis; bimekizumab for nail psoriasis

diseases.disease: Chronic Venous Insufficiency / Venous Leg Ulcers
diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Venous leg ulcers: spontaneous healing in 20-30% at 6 months without compression; recurrence in 70% within 1 year even after healing; CVI is progressive without intervention
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Compression therapy (graduated 40 mmHg): 70-80% healing at 24 weeks; venous ablation (EVLA, RFA) of incompetent veins + compression: 57% ulcer healing at 6 months vs 36% compression alone (VenUS IV); early intervention (within 6 months of ulcer onset) dramatically improves outcomes
diseases.gap_size: Large — venous ablation is significantly underused; compression adherence is the primary barrier in healed ulcers
diseases.barrier_detail: The Early Venous Reflux Ablation (EVRA) trial showed early ablation superior to compression-first strategy but most patients still wait 6+ months; compression stocking adherence is extremely poor without adequate education; many patients receive only wound dressings without compression or vascular assessment
diseases.last_soc_change: 2018 — EVRA trial published (early ablation as first-line for VLU with reflux); European guidelines 2015; compression as mainstay unchanged for decades; foam sclerotherapy/EVLA since early 2000s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Systemic antibiotics for non-infected VLU (routine use increases resistance, no healing benefit); betadine/povidone-iodine wound cleansing (cytotoxic to granulation tissue; not recommended in modern wound care); zinc paste bandages without reassessment for underlying venous incompetence
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: HBOT (hyperbaric oxygen) for non-healing VLU (some trial data — reimbursement variable); biofilm disruption agents (EDTA, PHMB); autologous platelet-rich plasma (PRP) for healing augmentation; negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) for debulking pre-compression
diseases.frontier_signal: Bioengineered skin substitutes (Apligraf, Dermagraft — established but underused); gene therapy for chronic wound healing (EGF gene delivery); adipose-derived stem cell application; extracellular matrix wound scaffolds

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Not applicable — surgical resection is standard; papillary microcarcinoma (<1 cm, low-risk): active surveillance achieves 99.8%+ cancer-specific survival at 10 years without immediate surgery in Japanese cohorts
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Total thyroidectomy + RAI for intermediate/high-risk: 90-95% disease-free survival at 10 years; active surveillance for low-risk papillary microcarcinoma: non-inferior to surgery in multiple cohorts; lenvatinib + pembrolizumab for RAI-refractory: OS benefit (LEAP-011 ongoing); vemurafenib for BRAF V600E mutant: response in 38%
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — overtreatment of low-risk disease is the dominant problem (too many total thyroidectomies in low-risk disease)
diseases.barrier_detail: The Kuma Hospital and Cancer Institute Hospital experience in Japan showing non-inferiority of active surveillance for low-risk papillary microcarcinoma has not been widely adopted outside Japan; total thyroidectomy with radioiodine for all differentiated thyroid cancer remains common despite lack of benefit in low-risk disease
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — ATA task force expanded active surveillance indications; lenvatinib now standard second-line; 2018 ATA guidelines reduced RAI use in low-risk; total thyroidectomy + RAI standard since 1990s (largely unchanged)
diseases.outdated_treatments: Total thyroidectomy + RAI for all papillary thyroid microcarcinomas (overtreatment; active surveillance non-inferior in low-risk); TSH suppression to very low levels in low-risk patients (increases cardiovascular and bone risk without cancer benefit); routine post-thyroidectomy calcium supplementation without individualised assessment
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Active surveillance for low-risk PTMC (expanding from Japan to US/Europe); selenium supplementation for Hashimoto's co-existing with differentiated thyroid cancer (reduces anti-TPO, may improve treatment tolerance); exercise during thyroid cancer treatment
diseases.frontier_signal: BRAF/MEK inhibitors for advanced BRAF V600E papillary thyroid cancer (dabrafenib + trametinib — FDA approved 2018 for other cancers, compassionate use thyroid cancer); RET inhibitors (selpercatinib, pralsetinib) for RET-mutant medullary thyroid cancer; NTRK inhibitors (larotrectinib) for NTRK-fusion thyroid cancers

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Without anticoagulation: DVT progresses to PE in 30-50%; massive PE has 30%+ mortality without treatment; spontaneous resolution of small PE in 30-40% but recurrence risk high
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban): equal or superior efficacy to warfarin with 50% reduction in major bleeding; catheter-directed thrombolysis for large DVT: rapid thrombus resolution but unclear long-term benefit on PTS (ATTRACT trial); acute massive PE with hemodynamic compromise: systemic thrombolysis reduces mortality 55-65%
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — DOACs have largely replaced warfarin; remaining gap is in chronic anticoagulation decision-making and post-VTE care
diseases.barrier_detail: DOAC superiority is established but warfarin persistence remains common in patients with mechanical valves (where DOACs are contraindicated) and in some LMIC settings; duration of anticoagulation after unprovoked VTE is an individualised decision poorly standardised; post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS) prevention with compression is rarely implemented
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — betrixaban/edoxaban expanding in specific populations; 2020 Andexanet alfa (reversal agent for FXa inhibitors) established; DOACs first-line since 2012-2014 (EINSTEIN, AMPLIFY trials); warfarin standard since 1954
