diseases.outdated_treatments: Prolonged bedrest after DVT (early ambulation with compression superior; bedrest increases PE risk and is now contraindicated); warfarin for uncomplicated DVT/PE where DOAC appropriate (inferior ease of use, bleeding risk, no reversal agent needed); IVC filter placement as primary VTE treatment (filters do not treat existing clot; anticoagulation superior in most cases)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Compression stockings for PTS prevention (ECS after DVT — IDEAL DVT trial suggests post-DVT compression reduces PTS 25-30%); aspirin for extended VTE prophylaxis after DOAC course (ASPIRE, WARFASA trials — significant recurrence reduction); early ambulation protocols
diseases.frontier_signal: Anti-XI-a antibodies (abelacimab, milvexian — prevent clotting without bleeding risk; Phase 3 AZALEA-TIMI 71 underway); Factor XIa inhibition paradigm shift; AI-based VTE risk scoring; direct reversible thrombin inhibitors without anticoagulation-related bleeding

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Essentially zero; progressive cytopaenia and transformation to AML in 30-40% of higher-risk MDS over 2-5 years; median survival low-risk MDS ~5 years, high-risk ~1 year without treatment
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Allogeneic HSCT: only curative option; 5-year OS 35-50% in fit patients; hypomethylating agents (azacitidine, decitabine): ORR 15-17%, overall survival improvement 9 months vs SC; oral azacitidine (CC-486): 10.7 vs 8.9 months OS in AML maintenance post-remission
diseases.gap_size: Large — MDS has limited treatment options; curative HSCT used in <15% of patients (age, comorbidity, donor availability); existing drugs are not curative
diseases.barrier_detail: MDS predominantly affects elderly patients who are often HSCT-ineligible; hypomethylating agents provide modest benefit without cure; luspatercept (TGF-β trap) approved for LR-MDS with ring sideroblasts 2020 — improved transfusion independence; imetelstat for LR-MDS — Phase 3 (IMerge) showed independence from transfusion in 40% vs 15% placebo
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — imetelstat (telomerase inhibitor) FDA approved for LR-MDS; luspatercept (2020) for RS+ LR-MDS; oral azacitidine (2020); parenteral azacitidine since 2004; EPO since mid-1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Danazol (androgens) for anaemia in LR-MDS (variable response, side effects; superseded by luspatercept/EPO); erythropoiesis-stimulating agents at high ESA doses without luspatercept or imetelstat consideration; watch-and-wait without transfusion thresholds or iron chelation monitoring
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Iron chelation with deferasirox in transfusion-dependent MDS (reduces iron overload, emerging OS data); oral luspatercept in clinical practice optimisation; exercise programme to maintain functional status in elderly MDS patients
diseases.frontier_signal: Imetelstat (first-in-class telomerase inhibitor — phase 3 positive 2023); sabatolimab (anti-TIM-3 immune checkpoint — Phase 3 ongoing); magrolimab (anti-CD47) + azacitidine; oral HMAs (novel decitabine formulations); venetoclax + azacitidine extension to MDS (VERMONT trial)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive; GFR decline averages 4-5 mL/min/year in Mayo Class 1C-1E; renal failure by 60-70 years in 50% without treatment; spontaneous stabilisation rare
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Tolvaptan (Jynarque): slows GFR decline by 35% and total kidney volume growth by 49% (TEMPO 3:4, REPRISE); dietary protein restriction + hydration: modest benefit; SGLT2 inhibitors — early signal in PKD trials
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — tolvaptan is available and effective but hepatotoxicity risk limits use; access barriers (REMS programme in US) reduce prescribing
diseases.barrier_detail: Tolvaptan REMS programme (liver toxicity monitoring) creates prescribing complexity; only patients with rapidly progressive disease (Mayo Class 1C-1E) are eligible by guideline; most patients remain on supportive care without disease-modifying therapy
diseases.last_soc_change: 2018 — tolvaptan FDA approved for ADPKD; TEMPO 3:4 published 2012; mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) tried and failed in RCTs 2010; supportive care unchanged for decades until tolvaptan
diseases.outdated_treatments: mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus/everolimus for ADPKD — Phase 3 failed, abandoned); routine caffeine restriction beyond adequate hydration (no evidence); aggressive aggressive blood pressure treatment to very low targets without individualised risk assessment
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Dietary: plain water intake >3L/day (reduces AVP stimulation, slows cyst growth — based on tolvaptan mechanism); sodium restriction; avoidance of nephrotoxins; SGLT2 inhibitors (early signal in ADPKD from Phase 2 trials — STAGED-PKD underway)
diseases.frontier_signal: Lixivaptan (V2R antagonist without hepatotoxicity, Phase 3 ALERT trial underway); bardoxolone methyl (NRF2 activation — Phase 3 CARDINAL Alport positive; ADPKD Phase 2 ongoing); mTOR inhibitors with tissue-specific targeting; targeted gene silencing (anti-PC2 siRNA in murine models)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous remission in ~10-15% over 2 years without treatment; IBM (inclusion body myositis) is slowly progressive with no effective treatment; death from interstitial lung disease in dermatomyositis in 5-10%
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: High-dose corticosteroids + IVIG + steroid-sparing agent (methotrexate/azathioprine): clinical remission in 40-60% of DM/PM; IVIG (ProQolity, FDA approved 2021 for DM): 79% response vs 44% placebo (PROPACK trial); rituximab in refractory: 77% response (RIM trial)
diseases.gap_size: Large for IBM (no effective treatment); moderate for DM/PM
diseases.barrier_detail: IBM (the most common myopathy in those >60 years) has no effective treatment despite multiple trial failures (bimagrumab, arimoclomol); DM/PM are managed with high-dose steroids first despite substantial side effects and emerging evidence for biologic first-line approaches
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — IVIG approved specifically for dermatomyositis (PROPACK Phase 3 — first RCT ever positive for any DM treatment); rituximab widely used off-label; corticosteroids as first-line since 1960s; EULAR/ACR classification criteria updated 2017
diseases.outdated_treatments: High-dose OCS monotherapy without early steroid-sparing agent introduction (standard of care but OCS dose should be tapered rapidly with steroid-sparing agent; many patients remain on high doses causing metabolic harm); plasma exchange (tried in PM/DM — not beneficial); malignancy screening non-systematic (missed paraneoplastic DM)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise rehabilitation in stable DM/PM (was contraindicated historically; now established as safe and beneficial); sun protection in dermatomyositis (UV light exacerbates DM skin disease); IVIG for acute flare management; physical therapy for IBM to maintain function
diseases.frontier_signal: Efgartigimod (FcRn inhibitor, reduces IgG including pathogenic autoantibodies — Phase 3 ALKIVIA for generalized MG extending to myositis); rapamycin/mTOR for IBM (sporadic IBM has mTOR dysregulation — Phase 2 ReDOS trial); bimagrumab (anti-ActRIIA/B) revisited for IBM; intravenous immunoglobulin optimisation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Patchy AA: spontaneous regrowth in 50-80% within 1 year; alopecia totalis/universalis (AT/AU): spontaneous full regrowth <10%; chronic AA (>10 years): spontaneous remission rare
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Baricitinib (Olumiant): 38.8% achieving ≥80% scalp coverage (SALT score ≤20) at 36 weeks vs 2% placebo (BRAVE-AA1); ritlecitinib (Litfulo): 23.4% SALT ≤10 at 24 weeks (ALLEGRO); dupilumab: negative Phase 3 for AA (not effective despite positive for AD)
diseases.gap_size: Very large — JAK inhibitors are transformative but no approved treatment existed before June 2022; most patients previously managed with immunosuppressants with low efficacy and side effects
diseases.barrier_detail: Baricitinib (2022) and ritlecitinib (2023) are the first approved treatments specifically for severe AA; prior to this, intralesional corticosteroids and systemic immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, methotrexate) provided variable and non-durable response; cost ($20-25K/year) and durability questions on treatment cessation are key barriers
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — ritlecitinib (Litfulo) FDA approved for AA (second JAK inhibitor); 2022 baricitinib (Olumiant) — first FDA-approved treatment for AA; prior systemic treatments used off-label
diseases.outdated_treatments: Systemic corticosteroids as primary treatment for AT/AU (temporary regrowth with rapid relapse; significant systemic side effects without disease modification); PUVA phototherapy (modest efficacy, carcinogenic risk, largely replaced by JAK inhibitors); contact sensitisation (DPCP application) — effective but cumbersome
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Scalp massage (promotes blood flow, some case reports); selenium (early signal in autoimmune hair loss); zinc supplementation (deficiency associated with AA — repletion may help); melatonin topical (very small studies); low-level laser therapy (LLLT — modest evidence for stimulating regrowth)
diseases.frontier_signal: Ritlecitinib (first JAK inhibitor approved specifically in AA — JAK3/TEC inhibitor, may have long-term durability advantage); deuruxolitinib (Phase 3 AA); brepocitinib (Phase 3); IL-4R blockade (dupilumab failed Phase 3 but IL-13-only targeting under study); stem cell-based follicular transplantation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous complete repigmentation extremely rare (<1%); partial spontaneous repigmentation possible in small lesions; progressive without treatment in most patients
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Ruxolitinib cream (Opzelura): 29.9% F-VASI75 (75% facial repigmentation) at 24 weeks vs 7.5% vehicle; NB-UVB phototherapy: 75%+ repigmentation in 30-40% of patients at 12 months; excimer laser for localised vitiligo: 75%+ repigmentation in 50-70%; combination ruxolitinib + NB-UVB: superior to either alone
diseases.gap_size: Large — first topical treatment only approved 2022; NB-UVB is effective but access to specialised units is limited
diseases.barrier_detail: Prior to ruxolitinib cream (2022), there was no FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for vitiligo; NB-UVB is effective but requires 2-3x/week sessions for months and is only available in dermatology/phototherapy units; cosmetic dismissal of vitiligo as 'not a medical disease' has delayed research and treatment access
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — ruxolitinib cream (Opzelura) FDA approved for non-segmental vitiligo — first ever approved pharmacotherapy; prior standard was off-label topical corticosteroids and NB-UVB
diseases.outdated_treatments: Depigmentation therapy (monobenzyl ether of hydroquinone) as first-line approach before attempting repigmentation (generally irreversible; should be last resort); systemic corticosteroids for extensive vitiligo (temporary arrest of spread, no repigmentation, systemic side effects; replaced by JAK inhibitor/NB-UVB combination)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: NB-UVB home phototherapy units (making treatment more accessible); topical tacrolimus for facial and sensitive-area vitiligo; afamelanotide (melanocyte-stimulating analogue) + NB-UVB combination (Phase 2 positive); dietary: antioxidants (beta-carotene, CoQ10, selenium — small trials)
diseases.frontier_signal: Ruxolitinib cream expanding access; tofacitinib topical (Phase 2); systemic JAK inhibitors for rapidly-spreading vitiligo (Phase 2 evidence); melanocyte transplantation (non-cultured epidermal suspension — effective for stable vitiligo); anti-IFN-γ approaches (target cytokine pathology in vitiligo)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous resolution in 50-60% of acute tinnitus (<3 months) without treatment; chronic tinnitus (>3 months): only 10-15% spontaneous resolution; habituated tinnitus (not distressing) in 75% at 5 years but sound remains in most
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT): 50-60% reduction in tinnitus-related distress (not sound elimination); tinnitus retraining therapy (TRT): 80% improvement in distress over 18 months; hearing aids for comorbid hearing loss: modest tinnitus reduction; sound therapy (masking): symptom relief without cure
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no pharmacological treatment exists; CBT and TRT are evidence-based but massively under-accessed
diseases.barrier_detail: No effective pharmacotherapy exists (multiple drug classes tried and failed); sound therapy and CBT are the only evidence-based interventions but trained tinnitus therapists are scarce; most patients are told to 'live with it'; digital CBT for tinnitus (iCBT) emerging as scalable solution
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — Lenire (bimodal neurostimulation device) cleared in some markets; 2022 FDA De Novo cleared for SAINT protocol-based devices; no pharmacotherapy approved; CBT/TRT standard since 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Vasodilators (ginkgo biloba, betahistine, nifedipine) prescribed for tinnitus (multiple RCTs negative; no vasoactive mechanism relevant in most tinnitus); hypnotherapy as primary intervention (limited evidence); antidepressants prescribed primarily for tinnitus sound rather than comorbid depression/anxiety
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Bimodal neurostimulation (Lenire — simultaneous auditory + tongue tip stimulation; Phase 3 TENT-A2 positive: 66% significant improvement vs 48% control); transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) targeting auditory cortex; closed-loop neurofeedback; acoustic coordinated reset neuromodulation (CR neuromodulation)
diseases.frontier_signal: Lenire (bimodal stimulation — expanding regulatory clearance); AM-101 (NMDA receptor antagonist, intratympanic — Phase 3 failed primary endpoint globally but some subgroups positive); rTMS; cochlear blood flow targeted therapies; molecular approaches to cochlear hair cell regeneration (Atoh1 gene delivery — may prevent tinnitus post-ototoxic drug)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Primary RLS is chronic; spontaneous remission in mild cases ~30-40% over 5 years; pregnancy-induced RLS remits post-delivery; augmentation on dopaminergic drugs worsens symptoms in 60-80% at 10 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Iron IV supplementation in iron-deficient RLS: 57% responders vs 35% placebo (ferric carboxymaltose — Phase 3 positive); alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin enacarbil, pregabalin): 53-55% RLS responders; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole): 70-80% initial response but augmentation in 60-80% at 10 years
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — iron deficiency treatment is underused as primary intervention; dopamine agonists chronically overused despite augmentation risk
diseases.barrier_detail: IV iron therapy for iron-deficient RLS (<50 µg/L ferritin) is highly effective and underused; dopamine agonists are first-line in most practices despite augmentation being essentially inevitable in long-term use; augmentation causes 24-hour symptoms worse than original disease
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — updated AAN/AASM guidelines recommending alpha-2-delta ligands over dopamine agonists as first-line (augmentation concern); ferric carboxymaltose approved for iron-deficient RLS; dopamine agonists (ropinirole, pramipexole) as first-line since 2006
diseases.outdated_treatments: Dopamine agonists as first-line for long-term RLS management (augmentation in 60-80%; guidelines now recommend alpha-2-delta ligands first-line for chronic RLS); benzodiazepines for RLS (clonazepam — no RCT evidence, tolerance/dependence risk); opioids as first-line (effective but should be reserved for refractory/augmented cases)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, ferumoxytol — evidence-based for iron-deficient RLS; dramatically underutilised); magnesium supplementation (modest RCT evidence, safe, commonly used); pneumatic compression devices (RLS relief mechanism: peripheral stimulation suppresses dorsal spinal cord excitability); dietary: ferritin-rich foods, avoid caffeine/alcohol
diseases.frontier_signal: Extended-release gabapentin enacarbil (Horizant — established but underutilised); opioid antagonist-augmented therapy (buprenorphine combinations); high-frequency spinal cord stimulation; transcranial magnetic stimulation; CRBN (cereblon) targeted protein degradation for dopaminergic normalisation

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Mild CTS: spontaneous resolution in 30-40% within 12 months without treatment; moderate-severe: <5% spontaneous resolution; pregnancy-related CTS resolves in 80% post-delivery
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Surgical decompression (carpal tunnel release, CTR): 90% symptom resolution at 12 months; 85% satisfaction at 5 years; superior to splinting at 6 months (SEVIORAL trial); corticosteroid injection: 75% improvement at 4 weeks, 50% at 6 months, 20% at 12 months
diseases.gap_size: Small — surgery is highly effective; the question is appropriate timing and selecting patients for non-surgical management
diseases.barrier_detail: Surgery is underused in some health systems where patients are kept on conservative management (splinting, cortisone) for years with progressive nerve damage; conversely, overused in mild cases where non-surgical management could suffice; ergonomic intervention is chronically under-addressed
diseases.last_soc_change: 2016 — AAOS CTS guideline updated; surgical release remains gold standard since 1970s; endoscopic vs open technique evidence established 2000s; no paradigm change
diseases.outdated_treatments: Vitamin B6 supplementation (consistent Cochrane review evidence of no benefit; still widely recommended and sold for CTS); diuretics for CTS (water-retaining theory largely abandoned; little evidence); open release in all patients regardless of severity (endoscopic offers equivalent outcomes with faster recovery in selected patients)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Ultrasound-guided hydrodissection with PRP/saline (emerging technique for releasing adhesions without surgery — Phase 2 data); therapeutic ultrasound (Cochrane review: limited evidence but some improvement in pain); nerve gliding exercises; ergonomic assessment and keyboard modification
diseases.frontier_signal: US-guided percutaneous needle hydrodissection (minimally invasive alternative to surgery — expanding Phase 2/3 data); steroid + hyaluronic acid injection combination; gene therapy for CRISPR-mediated median nerve regeneration (very early stage)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous fluctuation common; resolution in ~25% at 1 year; bladder training alone achieves 50-80% improvement in urgency frequency; OAB worsens with age without intervention
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Bladder training + pelvic floor exercises: 60-80% improvement (first-line guideline recommendation); anticholinergics/beta-3 agonist (mirabegron): 30-60% significant improvement; botulinum toxin A (OnabotulinumtoxinA, Botox): 30-50% complete continence at 12 weeks; sacral neuromodulation (InterStim): 72% success at 5 years
diseases.gap_size: Large — most patients receive pharmacotherapy as first-line without adequate behavioural therapy trial; anticholinergics overused in elderly
diseases.barrier_detail: Behavioural interventions (pelvic floor training, bladder diary, timed voiding) are first-line by all guidelines but most patients receive anticholinergics immediately; anticholinergic cognitive burden in elderly patients is significant (dementia risk association with chronic use — JAMA Intern Med 2019); mirabegron has better cognitive safety profile but is underused relative to older anticholinergics
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — AUA/SUFU OAB guideline updated; implantable tibial nerve stimulator (NURO) added 2019; onabotulinumtoxinA for OAB since 2013; mirabegron (Myrbetriq) 2012; anticholinergics (oxybutynin) since 1975
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term oxybutynin in elderly patients (high antimuscarinic burden — associated with cognitive decline, falls, dry mouth; STOPP criteria: inappropriate in dementia/cognitive impairment); immediate pharmacotherapy without adequately attempting bladder retraining; tolterodine without cognitive assessment in patients >65
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Pelvic floor physiotherapy (dramatically underutilised; equivalent to pharmacotherapy in RCTs for urgency incontinence); dietary: caffeine reduction (clear association with OAB symptoms), weight loss; tibial nerve stimulation (transcutaneous or percutaneous PTNS — office-based); low-frequency electrical stimulation
diseases.frontier_signal: Vibegron (beta-3 agonist — approved 2020; better CV safety than mirabegron for some patients); implantable tibial nerve stimulators (NURO — MOLC implant 2019); rechargeable InterStim (recharge interval extended); selective beta-3 agonists with fewer systemic effects; gene therapy for autonomic nerve regeneration in neurogenic OAB

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous passage without intervention: 70-80% of stones <4 mm; 40-50% of stones 4-6mm; <25% of stones >6mm; recurrence in 50% within 10 years without metabolic assessment and prevention
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin, 0.4mg): facilitate passage 30-45% faster for distal ureteral stones; ESWL: 75-80% stone-free for <2cm renal stones; PCNL: 90%+ stone-free for >2cm stones; dietary and metabolic prevention: 50% recurrence reduction with hydration + diet (STOP-JNKi-like approach)
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — metabolic evaluation and dietary prevention are highly effective but chronically underperformed after first stone
diseases.barrier_detail: Only 10-20% of first-time kidney stone patients receive a 24-hour urine metabolic evaluation that would guide specific dietary and pharmacological prevention; high fluid intake (>2.5L/day) is evidence-based and free but rarely systematically emphasised; recurrence is highly preventable with appropriate dietary modification
diseases.last_soc_change: 2019 — AUA/EAU guidelines updated on 24-hour urine evaluation (recommend for all recurrent stone formers, single stone in high-risk); ESWL vs URS evidence updated; no pharmacological paradigm change; thiazide diuretics and citrate supplementation standard since 1980s
diseases.outdated_treatments: ESWL for all stone types and locations (cystine, brushite, large stones — poor ESWL results; URS or PCNL preferred); routine urine culture without 24-hour metabolic evaluation in high-risk patients; opioid analgesia as first-line (NSAIDs proven equivalent and reduce stone passage time via ureteral muscle relaxation)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: High fluid intake (≥2.5L/day — reduces recurrence 50% as primary intervention); dietary counselling (low sodium, moderate calcium, low animal protein — prevents all major stone types); lemon juice (increases urinary citrate in hypocitraturia — comparable to potassium citrate in small trials); Finacea (azelaic acid) for uric acid stones
diseases.frontier_signal: Imaging-guided percutaneous lithotripsy with single-access approach expanding; lithotripsy gel phase change technology (prevents haematoma); robotic-assisted URS; CRISPR targeting SLC3A1 for cystinuria; vapour-based ablation for staghorn calculi

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Idiopathic gastroparesis: spontaneous resolution in ~30-40% at 5 years (NIDDK natural history study); diabetic gastroparesis: rarely remits, correlated with glycaemic control; postsurgical: highly variable
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Dietary modification + glycaemic optimisation: 50-60% improvement in symptoms; metoclopramide: 30-40% short-term symptom improvement (limited by side effects); gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra Therapy): 50-60% significant symptom improvement at 3-5 years in refractory patients; pyloric procedures (G-POEM): 75-80% short-term improvement
diseases.gap_size: Large — gastric electrical stimulation and pyloromyotomy are underused; metoclopramide misuse is the dominant pharmacotherapy problem
diseases.barrier_detail: Metoclopramide (the only FDA-approved drug for gastroparesis since 1979) carries a black box warning for tardive dyskinesia with >12 weeks use; yet many patients are maintained long-term; gastric electrical stimulation (GES) is underutilised and restricted to few centres; domperidone (off-label, superior safety) inaccessible in US except via FDA expanded access
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — G-POEM (gastric per-oral endoscopic pyloromyotomy) increasingly adopted; trazpiroben (Phase 3 GASS trial ongoing) and relamorelin (Phase 3 failed primary endpoint 2018) attempted; metoclopramide as sole approved drug since 1979 — one of the longest-unchanged pharmacotherapy landscapes in GI medicine
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term metoclopramide >12 weeks without alternative assessment (tardive dyskinesia risk — black box warning; yet chronic prescribing common); erythromycin IV as long-term oral agent (tolerance develops within days to weeks; useful for inpatient acute management not chronic); nasojejunal tube feeding without GES consideration in severe refractory cases
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Domperidone via FDA-compassionate use IND (superior safety profile vs metoclopramide; widely used outside US); dietary: small frequent low-fat, low-fibre meals; pyloric Botox injection (short-lived effect ~3 months; predicts G-POEM response); cannabis for nausea control in refractory cases (mixed evidence; legal-access dependent)
diseases.frontier_signal: G-POEM (endoscopic pyloromyotomy) — durable data accumulating; trazpiroben (D2/D3 antagonist + 5-HT4 agonist — Phase 3 GASS); velusetrag (selective 5-HT4 agonist — Phase 2 positive for symptom improvement); transcutaneous electrical acustimulation (TEA — Phase 2 data)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Partial spontaneous remission in 20-30% at 2 years; complete resolution rare; chronic pain condition with significant disability; no biomarker-defined diagnosis complicates studies
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Multimodal approach (dietary modification + physical therapy + neuromodulation + intravesical therapy): 50-70% overall treatment response but rarely complete remission; intravesical heparin + lidocaine + sodium bicarbonate: 70-80% short-term response; sacral neuromodulation: 48-60% significant improvement at 2 years; cyclosporine A (oral): 75% improvement in Hunner lesion IC vs 19% placebo
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no FDA-approved treatment; management is symptomatic and highly individualised
diseases.barrier_detail: Only pentosan polysulfate sodium (Elmiron, FDA approved 1996) has FDA approval for IC/BPS, and this is now associated with maculopathy (pigmentary maculopathy) with long-term use; Hunner lesion IC (25% of cases) is a distinct pathological entity with different treatment response than non-Hunner IC; diagnostic heterogeneity makes clinical trials extremely difficult
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — AUA IC/BPS guideline updated; Elmiron maculopathy warning strengthened; no new pharmacotherapy approval; pentosan polysulfate sodium only approved drug since 1996
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term pentosan polysulfate sodium without annual ophthalmology monitoring (maculopathy risk — discontinuation recommended if changes detected); intravesical DMSO without individualised assessment (effective in non-Hunner IC, potentially harmful in Hunner lesion IC); hydraulic distension (high-pressure) as diagnostic-therapeutic procedure — evidence weak, risk of bladder rupture
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Low-acid diet (IC diet — reduces urinary pH and bladder wall irritation; widely recommended though RCT evidence limited); pelvic floor physical therapy (strong evidence for IC/BPS with pelvic floor tension myalgia component); quercetin (anti-inflammatory supplement — small RCT positive); amitriptyline for central sensitisation component
diseases.frontier_signal: Cystoscopic fulguration of Hunner lesions (significant improvement in Hunner IC — gold standard for that subtype; expanding); AZD8835 (PI3Kδ/γ inhibitor Phase 2 for Hunner IC); spinal cord stimulation; CGRP antagonists for visceral pain sensitisation; stem cell therapy for glycosaminoglycan layer regeneration

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous complete stable remission (CSR) in 10-20% of patients over 10 years; generalised MG rarely resolves without treatment; ocular MG may remain limited in 50-60%
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Thymectomy in non-thymomatous MG at age <65: 50% more likely to achieve CSR at 3 years vs medical therapy (MGTX trial); eculizumab (ravulizumab): 40% reduction in exacerbations in AChR+ MG; efgartigimod alfa (Vyvgart): 67.7% MG-ADL responders (ADAPT trial); zilucoplan: 53% MG-ADL response (RAISE trial)
diseases.gap_size: Large — new complement inhibitors and FcRn antagonists represent genuine advances but cost and access are barriers; thymectomy underused in appropriate candidates
diseases.barrier_detail: Thymectomy (MGTX trial proven benefit) is delayed or not offered in many patients with non-thymomatous MG; eculizumab ($700K/year) dramatically limits access; efgartigimod and zilucoplan more accessible but still $200K+/year; corticosteroids cause significant morbidity in MG patients managed long-term
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — rozanolixizumab (Rystiggo, FcRn inhibitor) approved; zilucoplan (Zilbrysq, complement C5 inhibitor) 2023; efgartigimod alfa (Vyvgart) 2021; eculizumab 2017; thymectomy MGTX trial 2016; pyridostigmine/corticosteroids standard since 1950s-1960s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Plasma exchange or IVIG as long-term maintenance (appropriate for refractory crisis and pre-surgical preparation; not evidence-based for long-term maintenance vs immunosuppressive agents); long-term high-dose prednisolone without steroid-sparing agent; azathioprine without baseline TPMT activity testing (risk of severe leukopenia)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (previously contraindicated; now established as safe and beneficial for fatigue and function in stable MG); amifampridine (3,4-DAP) for LEMS (related neuromuscular junction disorder); energy conservation techniques; respiratory physiotherapy
diseases.frontier_signal: Rozanolixizumab (FcRn inhibitor — subcutaneous, reduces IgG including AChR antibodies); batoclimab (FcRn inhibitor — SC monthly); CAR-T cell therapy depleting pathogenic B cells (German case series extension to MG); anti-FcRn combination approaches; CRISPR for immune tolerance induction

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Essentially zero — uniformly progressive, fatal within 2-5 years median; <10% survive >10 years; spontaneous prolonged stabilisation in <1% (SOD1 heterozygous late-onset)
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Riluzole: 3-month median survival extension (modest); edaravone (Radicava): slows functional decline by 33% at 24 weeks in a specific ALS subgroup (ALSFRS-R); AMX0035 (sodium phenylbutyrate + taurursodiol): 6.5-month OS extension (CENTAUR trial — modest); tofersen (SOD1-ALS): 38% reduction in neurofilament light (biomarker); 2-year mortality benefit for SOD1-ALS
diseases.gap_size: Very large — ALS has no effective treatment; existing drugs provide modest or biomarker-based benefit; curative approaches are distant
diseases.barrier_detail: ALS is arguably the most therapeutically challenging neurodegenerative disease; 25+ Phase 3 trials have failed; tofersen (SOD1-targeting antisense oligonucleotide) represents the first mechanism-targeted approach and the most promising signal yet; multigenicity and sporadic forms make uniform treatment impossible
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — tofersen (Qalsody) FDA approved for SOD1-ALS based on neurofilament biomarker; AMX0035 (Relyvrio) approved 2022 — subsequently voluntary market withdrawal 2024 after Phase 3 PHOENIX trial failure; edaravone 2017; riluzole 1995
diseases.outdated_treatments: High-dose antioxidant cocktails (vitamin E, CoQ10 — multiple large trials negative); minocycline (neuroprotective, Phase 3 failed — possible harm); celecoxib + minocycline combinations (negative); lithium (Phase 3 failed despite positive pilot); stem cell treatments without evidence-based protocols
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Multidisciplinary ALS clinic care (demonstrated 7-month OS advantage vs standard neurology follow-up — one of the strongest intervention effects in ALS); NIV (non-invasive ventilation) extending survival by 7-11 months; percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) for nutrition; exercise — gentle, not strenuous
diseases.frontier_signal: Tofersen (SOD1-targeting ASO — first mechanism-targeted ALS drug; extending to pre-symptomatic prevention in SOD1 mutation carriers); C9orf72-targeting ASOs; FUS-targeting approaches; cell therapy (NurOwn stem cells — Phase 3 failed US); CRISPR base editing for ALS-causing mutations; stem-cell derived astrocyte replacement

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Inevitable progressive neurodegeneration in all carriers of HTT CAG expansion >36 repeats; no spontaneous remission; motor, cognitive, psychiatric decline universally progressive
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Tetrabenazine/deutetrabenazine (VMAT2 inhibitors): 25-30% reduction in chorea score (FIRST-HD); no disease-modifying treatment proven; genetic CSF biomarker (mutant huntingtin, mHTT) reduction achieved with tominersen ASO — but Phase 3 halted 2021 (higher dose harmful); lower-dose tominersen now in Phase 2/3 after exploratory biomarker signal
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no approved disease-modifying treatment; symptomatic treatment only
diseases.barrier_detail: HD is unique in having a perfectly predictive genetic test allowing pre-symptomatic intervention — the challenge is that trials of mHTT-lowering agents (tominersen, WVE-003) have faced setbacks; the therapeutic window (pre-manifest vs manifest disease) and appropriate dose remain unclear; genetic counselling for presymptomatic testing is complex
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — valbenazine (Ingrezza) exploring HD chorea; 2021 tominersen Phase 3 halted (unexpectedly worse in higher-dose arm); 2017 deutetrabenazine (Austedo) approved for HD chorea; 2008 tetrabenazine approved; no disease-modifying treatment since disease characterised
diseases.outdated_treatments: Haloperidol for chorea (D2 blockade reduces chorea but worsens rigidity in later stages; motor features better addressed with VMAT2 inhibitors); benzodiazepines for motor features without addressing psychiatric dimensions; long-term SSRIs without reassessment (depression in HD has specific neurobiological substrate)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise (ENROLL-HD exercise sub-study: physical activity associated with slower progression); multidisciplinary care (occupational therapy, speech pathology, physiotherapy — all evidence-based for maintaining function); genetic counselling for family planning; predictive testing support services
diseases.frontier_signal: WVE-003 (ASO targeting mutant allele while sparing wild-type — Phase 1/2 PRECISION-HD1 — mHTT lowering confirmed 2023); tominersen at low dose Phase 2/3 re-initiation; AMT-130 (AAV5-miHTT intrastriatally delivered gene silencing — Phase 1/2); ZF-HD (zinc finger protein repressor of HTT — Phase 1 initiated 2023); pridopidine (sigma-1R agonist — Phase 3 PROOF-HD ongoing)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Partial spontaneous improvement in ~25-30% over 5 years; complete resolution without treatment rare; chronic fluctuating course in majority; central sensitisation often persists
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Multimodal approach (aerobic exercise + CBT + sleep optimisation): most effective combination — 50-60% significant improvement; duloxetine: 50% pain response in 43% vs 29% placebo; milnacipran: similar; pregabalin: 50% pain response 38-50%; low-dose naltrexone (LDN): 30% improvement in open-label studies
diseases.gap_size: Large — pharmacotherapy is modestly effective; exercise is the most effective single intervention but chronically underused as 'too painful'; disease is often dismissed
diseases.barrier_detail: Fibromyalgia was dismissed as non-organic by mainstream medicine for decades; this has resulted in: delayed diagnosis (mean 5 years), inappropriate investigations, ineffective treatments (opioids in particular), and under-referral to physiotherapy and psychological therapies; the 2010 ACR criteria removed tender point requirement and improved diagnosis
diseases.last_soc_change: 2021 — 2021 update to EULAR fibromyalgia recommendations; milnacipran/duloxetine/pregabalin approvals 2007-2008; these remain the only FDA-approved drugs for fibromyalgia; tricyclics (amitriptyline) off-label since 1980s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term opioid therapy for fibromyalgia (multiple guidelines explicitly contraindicate; worsens central sensitisation and pain thresholds; yet still common — 34% of FM patients on opioids in US); corticosteroids (not indicated; inflammatory basis not proven); muscle relaxants long-term (modest evidence, significant sedation/dependence potential)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) 1.5-4.5mg — Phase 2 data showing ~30% pain reduction; mechanism: microglial activation blockade; magnesium malate supplementation (some trial data); S-adenosyl methionine (SAMe) — systematic review positive; transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) — Phase 2 positive; whole-body cryotherapy (emerging evidence)
diseases.frontier_signal: Nalmefene (kappa opioid receptor modulation); sodium oxybate (Xyrem for sleep restoration in FM — Phase 3 TONIX failed; sodium oxybate/FMX-8 formulation — Phase 3 REST positive 2023); ketamine IV for refractory FM pain; low-field magnetic stimulation (sTMS); psilocybin for central sensitisation (Phase 2 planned)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous regression documented in ~0.4% of metastatic melanoma cases; early-stage surgical resection: 90%+ 5-year survival for Stage I; Stage IV (metastatic): historically <10% 5-year survival
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Stage IV with PD-1/CTLA-4 combination (nivolumab + ipilimumab): 52% 5-year OS (CheckMate-067); BRAF V600E + MEK inhibition (dabrafenib + trametinib): 34% 5-year OS; pembrolizumab adjuvant Stage III: 58.4% vs 45.9% RFS at 5 years; TIL therapy (Amtagvi): 31.5% ORR in heavily pretreated patients
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — genuine cures are now achievable for many Stage IV melanoma patients; the question is maximising who can access these outcomes
diseases.barrier_detail: Immunotherapy combination (nivolumab + ipilimumab) achieves durable responses in ~25-30% of Stage IV patients (functional cure); BRAF-targeted therapy is highly effective in BRAF V600E (50% of melanomas) but resistance develops in most; tumour-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy (Amtagvi, 2024) approved — cell manufacturing is complex
diseases.last_soc_change: 2024 — Amtagvi (lifileucel, TIL therapy) FDA approved — first tumour-infiltrating lymphocyte cell therapy approved for any cancer; 2020 adjuvant pembrolizumab Stage IIB-III; 2011 ipilimumab (first checkpoint inhibitor approved for any cancer); PD-1 inhibitors since 2014
diseases.outdated_treatments: Dacarbazine chemotherapy as primary treatment for Stage IV without immunotherapy or targeted therapy assessment (historical standard; response rate <15%, no OS benefit; completely superseded by immunotherapy for eligible patients); high-dose IL-2 (response rate similar to modern IO but extreme toxicity; now rarely used outside of IL-2-responder prediction platforms)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: mRNA personalised therapeutic vaccines (mRNA-4157/V940 + pembrolizumab: 65% reduction in recurrence risk at 3 years in resected Stage III/IV — KEYNOTE-942); exercise during checkpoint inhibitor therapy (reduces immune-related adverse events); Mediterranean diet; UV photoprotection for skin surveillance
diseases.frontier_signal: mRNA personalised cancer vaccines (mRNA-4157 — Phase 3 KEYNOTE-942A underway after remarkable Phase 2 data); TIL expansion approaches (Amtagvi expanding to adjuvant setting); anti-LAG-3 + anti-PD-1 (relatlimab — Opdualag approved 2022); intratumoral injection approaches; CAR-T for metastatic melanoma

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Essentially zero; one of the highest cancer mortality rates; 5-year survival ~12% overall; only 15-20% have resectable disease at diagnosis
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Resectable: surgery (Whipple) + FOLFIRINOX adjuvant: 54.4 months median OS (PRODIGE 24, vs 35.0 months gemcitabine); BRCA-mutant metastatic: olaparib maintenance after platinum-based: 7.4 vs 3.8 months PFS (POLO); neoadjuvant chemotherapy: may convert 20-30% of borderline-resectable to resectable
diseases.gap_size: Very large — minimal survival improvement for most patients over past 20 years; imaging and liquid biopsy for early detection is the biggest opportunity
diseases.barrier_detail: Pancreatic cancer is detected late (70-80% at Stage III-IV) due to absence of early symptoms and screening; CA19-9 is insufficiently sensitive/specific for early detection; BRCA1/2 and other HRR mutation testing is not universal; KRAS G12D/G12V targeted agents represent a genuine pipeline advance
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — NALIRIFOX first-line for metastatic PDAC (NAPOLI-3 — modest improvement); 2020 olaparib maintenance for BRCA-mutant; 2019 FOLFIRINOX adjuvant standard; gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel first-line since 2013; gemcitabine monotherapy since 1997
diseases.outdated_treatments: 5-FU monotherapy (replaced by gemcitabine since 1997, yet still seen as palliative option); biliary stent without concurrent systemic therapy in borderline-resectable (multidisciplinary evaluation mandatory before stenting in surgically eligible patients); COX-2 inhibitor chemoprevention (observational data; not proven in RCTs)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Weight maintenance and muscle preservation during chemotherapy (sarcopenia worsens OS and treatment tolerance); palliative care early integration (improves QoL and OS in metastatic PDAC — parallel to lung cancer data); CA-19-9 monitoring combined with ctDNA for response assessment
diseases.frontier_signal: KRAS G12D/G12V inhibitors (MRTX1133 — Phase 1/2 underway; KRAS mutant in 90% of PDAC); RAS-targeted therapies expanding from lung (sotorasib/adagrasib for G12C, <1% PDAC); mRNA personalised cancer vaccines (mRNA-4157 Phase 2 data in resected PDAC — V940-001); claudin 18.2-targeting (zolbetuximab — Phase 3 SPOTLIGHT/GLOW data extending to PDAC); immune-suppressive stroma targeting approaches

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: No spontaneous remission; 5-year survival Stage I: 90%; Stage III/IV: 25-30%; most patients (75%) present at Stage III-IV
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Debulking surgery + carboplatin/paclitaxel: 70-80% initial response; PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib) maintenance in BRCA-mutant: 56% 5-year PFS vs 13.8% placebo (SOLO-1); bevacizumab maintenance: modest PFS benefit; dose-dense paclitaxel: 72.1% vs 65.1% OS at 3 years
diseases.gap_size: Large — PARP inhibitors dramatically improve outcomes in BRCA-mutant patients; HRD testing not universal; no effective screening exists
diseases.barrier_detail: BRCA1/2 and HRD testing is not universal despite major impact on PARP inhibitor eligibility; bevacizumab access limited by cost; most patients are diagnosed at advanced stage due to no screening modality; CA-125 + transvaginal ultrasound screening trials (UKCTOCS) failed to show mortality reduction
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — mirvetuximab soravtansine (IMGN853, FRα-ADC) approved for FRα-high platinum-resistant OC; 2022 olaparib maintenance expanded; 2018 PARP inhibitor maintenance established; carboplatin/paclitaxel standard since 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: CA-125 alone for monitoring without imaging integration; routine second-look laparoscopy (abandoned — clinical outcomes not improved despite resection at second-look); routine topotecan in all platinum-resistant before HER-targeted or FRα assessment
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Exercise during chemotherapy (reduces platinum-related neurotoxicity); intraperitoneal chemotherapy for optimal cytoreduction (HIPEC — OVHIPEC-2 trial completed — conflicting data on benefit); dietary fat reduction (observational data); PARP inhibitor combination strategies
diseases.frontier_signal: Mirvetuximab soravtansine (FRα-ADC — approved 2022 for platinum-resistant); antibody-drug conjugates expanding; WEE1 inhibitor (adavosertib — Phase 2 data); anti-VEGF next generation; PARP inhibitor + IO (olaparib + pembrolizumab — KEYLYNK-001 Phase 3)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: NMIBC: 50-70% recurrence within 5 years; MIBC: progression to metastatic in 50% without radical treatment; spontaneous remission essentially zero
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: NMIBC: BCG intravesical immunotherapy: 70% reduction in recurrence, 30-40% reduction in progression; MIBC with neoadjuvant cisplatin + radical cystectomy: 5-year survival 50-60% (vs 45% cystectomy alone); metastatic with enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab: 31.5 months OS vs 16.1 months cisplatin (EV-302/KEYNOTE-A39)
diseases.gap_size: Large — EV + pembrolizumab is a major advance but not yet universally standard; BCG supply shortage is a persistent clinical crisis in NMIBC
diseases.barrier_detail: BCG shortage (since 2012, persistent) means many NMIBC patients cannot receive standard treatment; gemcitabine + mitomycin as alternative to BCG established as FDA-cleared 2024 (CISTO trial); EV + pembrolizumab is a genuine paradigm change for metastatic disease but requires PD-L1 testing integration
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — EV + pembrolizumab (EV-302) established as first-line for cisplatin-eligible metastatic UC; 2022 pembrolizumab second-line established; 2019 enfortumab vedotin approved; BCG standard for NMIBC since 1990
diseases.outdated_treatments: MVAC chemotherapy in cisplatin-eligible patients (gemcitabine + cisplatin equivalent efficacy with better tolerability — largely replaced but MVAC persists in some centres); maintenance BCG without checking BCG availability and dosing schedule; radical cystectomy without neoadjuvant chemotherapy discussion in MIBC
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Bladder preservation (trimodal therapy — maximal TURBT + chemoradiation): 5-year survival equivalent to cystectomy in selected patients; exercise and stomal care after cystectomy; FGFR inhibitors (erdafitinib — FGFR-altered UC)
diseases.frontier_signal: Enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab becoming first-line standard; sacituzumab govitecan for UC (TROPHY-U-01 Phase 2 positive); erdafitinib (FGFR2/3 targeted — Phase 3 THOR positive vs chemo in FGFR-altered); CLDN18.2 targeting; TAR-200 (intravesical gemcitabine releasing device for BCG-unresponsive NMIBC)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: CIN2/3 (precancerous): spontaneous regression in 40-60% of CIN2 without treatment; CIN3: regression in ~30%; invasive cervical cancer: no spontaneous regression
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Early-stage (IA-IIA): radical hysterectomy or chemoradiation — 80-90% 5-year survival; locally advanced (IIB-IVA): cisplatin-based chemoradiation — 60-70% 5-year survival; metastatic with pembrolizumab + chemotherapy ± bevacizumab: 24.4 vs 16.3 months OS (KEYNOTE-826); ENGOT-cx11/GOG-3047: cemiplimab + chemo superior
diseases.gap_size: Very large from a prevention standpoint — HPV vaccination and screening can prevent most cervical cancers but coverage is dramatically insufficient
diseases.barrier_detail: HPV vaccination prevents ~90% of cervical cancers caused by HPV 16/18 (and gardasil 9 covers 9 types); cervical cancer screening (Pap + HPV co-testing) prevents invasive cancer; yet globally only 10% of women in LMICs have received the HPV vaccine; in the US, HPV vaccination coverage remains at ~60% of adolescents
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — cemiplimab approved as first-line for PD-L1+ advanced/metastatic CC; 2021 pembrolizumab as first-line (KEYNOTE-826); tisotumab vedotin (ADC) 2021; cisplatin chemoradiation as standard since 1999
diseases.outdated_treatments: Radical hysterectomy for locally advanced disease without chemoradiation (chemoradiation superior for stage IIB+); routine ovarian transposition in young patients for all locally advanced cases (individual assessment needed); delayed initiation of pembrolizumab while continuing bevacizumab alone in metastatic PD-L1+ disease
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: HPV vaccination as prevention (catch-up to age 45); primary HPV testing replacing cytology (more sensitive for early detection); self-collected HPV samples (enabling screening in resource-limited settings); immune modulation strategies for CIN2/3 (topical imiquimod — Phase 2 data)
diseases.frontier_signal: Tisotumab vedotin (HER2-ADC targeting tissue factor — Phase 3 innovaTV 301 positive); CXCL10/IP-10 pathway targeting; therapeutic HPV vaccines (ISA101, MEDI0457 — Phase 2 data); CAR-T targeting HPV oncoproteins E6/E7; self-sampling HPV testing expansion (eliminates cervical screening access barrier)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: PV/ET/MF: no spontaneous remission; progressive disorders with risk of transformation to MF (PV/ET) and blast phase (all); median survival PV >20 years, MF 5-7 years
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: JAK2 inhibitor (ruxolitinib) for MF/PV: 35-42% spleen volume reduction, significant symptom improvement; ropeginterferon alfa-2b: superior complete haematological response vs hydroxyurea (PROUD-PV); allogeneic HSCT (MF): curative in 30-60% of transplant-eligible patients
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — ruxolitinib is established but HSCT underused in eligible MF patients; ropeginterferon is superior in PV but access varies
diseases.barrier_detail: Hydroxyurea remains the most common cytoreductive agent for PV/ET despite ropeginterferon demonstrating superior molecular response; HSCT for MF is underutilised in patients who could benefit; JAK2 mutation molecular monitoring not performed systematically
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — ropeginterferon alfa-2b (Besremi) FDA approved for PV; 2019 fedratinib (JAK2/FLT3) for MF; pacritinib 2022 for thrombocytopenic MF; ruxolitinib 2011 for MF, 2014 for PV; hydroxyurea standard since 1960s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Phlebotomy alone for all PV without cytoreductive therapy in high-risk patients (standard in low-risk but insufficient in high-risk for thrombosis prevention); chlorambucil/busulfan for PV cytoreduction (leukaemogenic; replaced by hydroxyurea or interferon); anagrelide in all ET patients without thrombosis risk stratification
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Aspirin (low-dose — reduces thrombosis risk in PV; established since ECLAP trial 2004, yet not universally prescribed); cardiovascular risk factor optimisation; hematocrit <45% maintenance (CYTO-PV trial — critical for thrombosis risk reduction)
diseases.frontier_signal: Pelabresib (BET inhibitor + ruxolitinib for MF — Phase 3 MANIFEST-2 positive 2023); navitoclax (BCL2/BCL-XL inhibitor + ruxolitinib — Phase 3 TRANSFORM-1 positive 2023); luspatercept for MF-related anaemia; CAR-T targeting CD123 in blast phase MPNs; luspatercept in PV for iron management

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive exocrine and endocrine insufficiency; spontaneous improvement possible in alcoholic CP with sustained abstinence (30-40% pain improvement); structural damage (fibrosis, calcifications) is irreversible
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Abstinence from alcohol in alcoholic CP: 30-40% pain improvement; endoscopic drainage of ductal obstruction: pain relief in 65-70% at 5 years; total pancreatectomy with islet autotransplantation (TPIAT): 85-90% narcotic independence at 3 years in refractory cases; antioxidant therapy: modest evidence
diseases.gap_size: Large — endoscopic therapy and surgery are underused in eligible patients; most are managed with analgesics indefinitely
diseases.barrier_detail: Pain management in CP often escalates to opioids without adequate trial of endoscopic decompression or TPIAT in eligible patients; TPIAT (total pancreatectomy + islet autotransplantation) for refractory pain is highly effective but available at only ~20 centres in the US; delayed referral is the dominant gap
diseases.last_soc_change: 2018 — AGA/ACG chronic pancreatitis guidelines updated; endoscopic therapy (ESWL + endoscopy) vs surgery (ESCAPE trial 2020) — surgery superior at 5 years for painful CP with duct dilatation; no major pharmacotherapy change in decades
diseases.outdated_treatments: Long-term opioid analgesia for CP pain without endoscopic assessment (escalates dependence without addressing ductal hypertension cause in duct-obstructed patients); oral enzyme supplementation at sub-therapeutic doses (needs 50,000+ units lipase per meal); antioxidant cocktails without individualised nutritional assessment
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) optimisation for exocrine insufficiency; smoking cessation (accelerates CP progression; underemphasised); medium-chain triglyceride diet for fat malabsorption; CGRP antagonists for visceral pain component; coeliac plexus blockade (short-term pain relief bridge)
diseases.frontier_signal: TPIAT expansion (more centres; cell banking approaches); extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) optimisation for calcific CP; islet cell transplantation from donor pancreas for post-pancreatitis diabetes; anti-fibrotic agents (pirfenidone — Phase 2 for pancreatic fibrosis); stellate cell-targeted therapy

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Childhood ITP: spontaneous remission in 70-80% within 6 months; Adult ITP: spontaneous remission in 20-30% at 1 year without treatment; chronic ITP (>12 months) rarely resolves spontaneously
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Rituximab: 40-60% initial response, 20-25% durable at 5 years; thrombopoietin receptor agonists (TPO-RA: eltrombopag, romiplostim): 70-90% response rate; splenectomy: 60-70% long-term complete response; fostamatinib: 18% CR in refractory; avatrombopag and lusutrombopag in perioperative settings
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — TPO-RAs are highly effective but chronic use required in most; splenectomy (durable cure in 65%) is underused in appropriate patients
diseases.barrier_detail: Splenectomy offers durable cure in 65% of ITP patients but is increasingly deferred in favour of chronic TPO-RA therapy which requires lifelong administration; the optimal sequence (corticosteroids → rituximab → TPO-RA → splenectomy) is debated; overtreated mild cases (platelet >30K without bleeding) remain a clinical challenge
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — rilzabrutinib (BTK inhibitor) Phase 3 LUNA data; daprodustat + avatrombopag combinations; 2021 fostamatinib (SYK inhibitor) added; 2008 romiplostim/eltrombopag first-line TPO-RAs; splenectomy standard since early 20th century
diseases.outdated_treatments: Danazol for chronic ITP (androgenic side effects; modest efficacy; dexamethasone or rituximab preferred); long-term OCS without steroid-sparing agent (prolonged corticosteroid use in ITP causes substantial morbidity); vinca alkaloids (vincristine/vinblastine) as emergency treatment (IV TPO-RA and IVIG preferred in acute setting)
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: H. pylori eradication in H. pylori-positive ITP (remission in 30-50% — underperformed globally); low-dose naltrexone (LDN) in immune dysregulation; TPO-RA tapering protocols for drug-free remission induction
diseases.frontier_signal: Rilzabrutinib (BTK inhibitor — Phase 3 data; reversible inhibitor with better platelet function profile than ibrutinib); iptacopan (complement factor B inhibitor — Phase 3 APLASTIC ANEMIA; ITP Phase 2); anti-CD38 (mezagitamab) for refractory ITP; CAR-T targeting pathogenic autoimmune B cells

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous remission at 1 year: ~50%; at 5 years: ~65-70%; but 10-15% remain chronic beyond 5 years; remission followed by relapse common
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Omalizumab (anti-IgE): complete symptom resolution in 36-44% at 12 weeks (GLACIAL, ASTERIA trials); high-dose antihistamines (4x standard dose): 50-60% symptom control; cyclosporine for antihistamine-refractory: 50-60% response; dupilumab for CSU: 73% UAS7 ≤6 at 24 weeks (Phase 3 LIBERTY-CSU CUPID 2023)
diseases.gap_size: Moderate — omalizumab is transformative but not accessible as first-line; many patients on inadequate antihistamine doses or antihistamine monotherapy long-term
diseases.barrier_detail: Omalizumab (Xolair) was FDA-approved for CSU in 2014 but remains unavailable as first-line (prior authorisation requiring antihistamine failure first); many patients spend years on sedating antihistamines or systemic OCS; dupilumab (2023 Phase 3 data) adds a second biologic option
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — dupilumab Phase 3 LIBERTY-CSU CUPID positive (likely approval pending); 2014 omalizumab approved for CSU; cetirizine/fexofenadine as first-line since 1990s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Hydroxyzine as first-line in non-elderly patients (sedating antihistamine; non-sedating preferred; impairs cognitive function, driving); long-term systemic corticosteroids for CSU (effective short-term for acute flares only; significant morbidity with chronic use); routine cold allergy testing for chronic urticaria without dermatographism
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Stress management (CSU is stress-responsive — lifestyle interventions); vitamin D optimisation (deficiency associated with CSU; repletion may improve response to antihistamines); dietary low-pseudoallergen diet (in selected food-triggered CSU); H2 antihistamine addition (famotidine) to H1 as adjunct for gastric mast cells
diseases.frontier_signal: Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα — Phase 3 CSU positive 2023); remibrutinib (BTK inhibitor — Phase 3 REMIX-1/2 ongoing for CSU); ligelizumab (next-gen anti-IgE — Phase 3 failed vs omalizumab but subgroup activity); anti-Siglec-8 (lirentelimab); IgE pathway targeting combinations

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Refractory coeliac (Type I, II): extremely poor prognosis; RCD-II: 5-year survival 50-58%; GI-lymphoma risk substantial; RCD-I may improve with strict GFD in ~50%
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: RCD-I: strict GFD ± steroids: 50% histological response; RCD-II: corticosteroids/azathioprine: partial response; cladribine + autologous SCT: some long-term remissions in RCD-II; tofacitinib: Phase 2 positive (TILDA trial)
diseases.gap_size: Very large — RCD is rare but without effective treatment; distinction from standard coeliac is critical
diseases.barrier_detail: RCD represents a subset of coeliac disease with abnormal intraepithelial lymphocytes that has progressed beyond GFD responsiveness; RCD-II is a pre-lymphoma condition with very poor prognosis; lack of specialised centres for diagnosis and management; JAK inhibitor use represents a genuine therapeutic advance
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — tofacitinib (JAK1/3 inhibitor) Phase 2 TILDA trial positive for RCD-II; no approved pharmacotherapy for RCD; corticosteroids off-label since 2000s
diseases.outdated_treatments: Elemental diet as primary treatment (extremely restrictive, no clear advantage over GFD + medical treatment); steroids without nutritional assessment; open (not TCR-gamma/delta rearrangement tested) monitoring without endoscopic surveillance
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Systematic endoscopic surveillance for RCD-II given lymphoma risk; nutritional rehabilitation; latiglutenase supplementation to protect against inadvertent gluten exposure; psychosocial support for dietary adherence in a condition requiring complete gluten elimination even when GFD has failed
diseases.frontier_signal: Tofacitinib for RCD-II (Phase 2 positive TILDA); TE-701 (ZED1227 — TG2 inhibitor — may protect against ongoing immune activation even with trace gluten); HLA-DQ2/DQ8 blocking peptides; ALX-0171 (nanobody blocking IgA anti-TG2 — Phase 1/2)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive lung and liver disease; ZZ genotype: FEV1 decline accelerated (80-100 mL/year vs 30 mL/year in non-smokers); liver cirrhosis progressive; no spontaneous resolution without augmentation therapy
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Alpha-1 augmentation therapy (IV human AAT): slows CT-measured emphysema progression by 34% (RAPID trial); smoking cessation: most important modifiable factor (reduces FEV1 decline to near-normal rate); fazirsiran (RNAi targeting hepatic AAT production): 83% reduction in Z-AAT liver accumulation
diseases.gap_size: Very large — only 5-10% of AATD individuals are diagnosed; augmentation therapy reduces CT progression but functional benefit debated; liver disease has no approved disease-modifying therapy
diseases.barrier_detail: AATD is misdiagnosed as COPD or asthma for a mean of 5-7 years; spirometry for COPD should trigger AATD testing but this is rarely done; fazirsiran (RNAi) reduces hepatic Z-protein accumulation — Phase 3 underway for liver disease; augmentation therapy is IV every 4 weeks lifelong (~$100K/year)
diseases.last_soc_change: 2022 — Phase 3 ATIXA trial of fazirsiran ongoing; RAPID trial results (2015) established augmentation therapy; IV augmentation therapy approved 1987; no major change in lung AATD management for 35 years
diseases.outdated_treatments: Treating AATD-associated emphysema as standard COPD without augmentation consideration; delayed AATD testing in young-onset COPD or COPD in never-smokers; plasma-derived augmentation without genetic confirmation of ZZ/SZ genotype
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Smoking cessation (paramount — normalises FEV1 decline); pulmonary rehabilitation; liver transplantation for end-stage AATD liver disease (curative for liver, does not improve lung); screening family members of diagnosed probands
diseases.frontier_signal: Fazirsiran (ARO-AAT, RNAi — Phase 3 SEQUOIA trial for liver disease); belcesiran (ARO-AAT2); gene therapy (AAV-AAT gene delivery to liver and lung — Phase 2, InVivo Therapeutics); CRISPR correction of Z-allele in liver organoids (preclinical); small molecule correctors of Z-AAT misfolding (C-type chaperones)

diseases.spontaneous_remission_rate: Progressive cholestatic disease; median time to liver failure or transplant 12-15 years; spontaneous remission essentially zero; biliary strictures progress in majority; cholangiocarcinoma risk 10-15% lifetime
diseases.best_intervention_remission_rate: Liver transplantation: 5-year survival 80-85% (curative but recurrence in 20% post-transplant); ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA): improvement in LFTs but no transplant-free survival benefit (Phase 3 — no benefit, possibly harm at high dose); no pharmacotherapy proven to alter the natural history
diseases.gap_size: Very large — no approved disease-modifying therapy exists; liver transplantation is the only definitive treatment
diseases.barrier_detail: PSC has resisted all pharmacotherapy attempts; UDCA widely prescribed despite no survival benefit (at high doses — possibly harmful); the field has 20+ failed Phase 2/3 trials; norUDCA, nor-UDCA, and several FXR agonists are in Phase 3; gut-liver axis and gut microbiome increasingly implicated (80% of PSC patients have concurrent IBD)
diseases.last_soc_change: 2023 — multiple Phase 3 trials underway but no approval; UDCA re-evaluated (Phase 3 Mayo trial showed no benefit); annual colon and biliary cancer surveillance as major guideline update; no approved pharmacotherapy to date
diseases.outdated_treatments: High-dose UDCA (25-30 mg/kg/day) for PSC (Phase 3 trial showed increased risk of varices and transplant at high dose — 2010 Mayo trial; yet still prescribed in many centres); empirical antibiotics without biliary infection confirmation; endoscopic dilation of dominant strictures without histological sampling for cholangiocarcinoma
diseases.adjunctive_emerging: Gut microbiome modulation (IBD treatment optimisation may slow PSC progression); vancomycin oral (VINTAGE trial — positive pilot; Phase 3 ongoing for paediatric PSC; mechanism: gut microbiome shift); fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K); dietary: low-fat diet for malabsorption
diseases.frontier_signal: Nor-ursodeoxycholic acid (norUDCA) — Phase 3 NordISC trial (positive interim data — first PSC drug showing ALP normalisation); cilofexor (FXR agonist) — Phase 3 data; nitazoxanide (gut parasite + immunomodulatory — Phase 2 MaRIA-PSC positive); BTK inhibitors for biliary inflammation; fecal microbiota transplantation (Phase 2 MELT trial)
